# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Define a fuzzy relation. How does it differ from a classical (crisp) relation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A fuzzy relation $R$ on $X \times Y$ is a mapping $\mu_R : X \times Y \rightarrow [0, 1]$ that assigns to each ordered pair $(x, y)$ a real number in $[0, 1]$ representing the strength of the relationship between $x$ and $y$.

A classical (crisp) relation is a special case of a fuzzy relation where the codomain is restricted to $\{0, 1\}$ — every pair is either related or not, with no graded strength. Fuzzy relations allow continuous degrees of relatedness, making them suitable for modelling real-world imprecision.

*(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: crisp relation note, 1 mark: key difference)*

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

> **State and explain the max–min composition of two fuzzy relations with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Let $R$ be a fuzzy relation on $X \times Y$ and $S$ be a fuzzy relation on $Y \times Z$. Their max–min composition $T = R \circ S$ is a fuzzy relation on $X \times Z$ defined by:

$$\mu_{R \circ S}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y}\;\min\bigl(\mu_R(x, y),\; \mu_S(y, z)\bigr)$$

**Example:** For $R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$ and $S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.6 \\ 0.8 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$, the entry $\mu_{R \circ S}(x_1, z_1) = \max(\min(0.5, 0.4), \min(0.3, 0.8)) = \max(0.4, 0.3) = 0.4$.

*(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: formula, 1 mark: numerical illustration)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question B — Module 2 Choice. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Pattern]

**Question A (14 Marks) — Operations, Product, and Composition**

**(a)** Let $A = \{(x_1, 0.3),\; (x_2, 0.8)\}$ and $B = \{(y_1, 0.5),\; (y_2, 0.6),\; (y_3, 0.2)\}$ be two fuzzy sets. Compute the Cartesian product $A \times B$ using the min operator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the two fuzzy relations:

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.6 \\ 0.1 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix} \quad (X \times Y), \quad S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.7 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix} \quad (Y \times Z)$$

Compute the max–min composition $R \circ S$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Lay out the universes.**
$X = \{x_1, x_2\}$ and $Y = \{y_1, y_2, y_3\}$. So the result is a $2 \times 3$ matrix.

**Step 2 — Apply the min rule entry-wise, $\mu_{A \times B}(x, y) = \min(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(y))$.**

Row for $x_1$ ($\mu_A(x_1) = 0.3$):
$$\min(0.3, 0.5) = 0.3, \quad \min(0.3, 0.6) = 0.3, \quad \min(0.3, 0.2) = 0.2$$

Row for $x_2$ ($\mu_A(x_2) = 0.8$):
$$\min(0.8, 0.5) = 0.5, \quad \min(0.8, 0.6) = 0.6, \quad \min(0.8, 0.2) = 0.2$$

**Step 3 — Final matrix:**

$$A \times B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.3 & 0.2 \\ 0.5 & 0.6 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Mark distribution: 1 mark for setup, 4 marks for entry-wise calculation, 2 marks for correct final matrix.*

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify the result dimension.** $R$ is $2 \times 2$, $S$ is $2 \times 2$, so $R \circ S$ is $2 \times 2$ on $X \times Z$.

**Step 2 — Compute the four entries using $\mu_{R \circ S}(x_i, z_j) = \max_y \min(\mu_R(x_i, y), \mu_S(y, z_j))$.**

**Entry $(x_1, z_1)$:**
- via $y_1$: $\min(0.4, 0.5) = 0.4$
- via $y_2$: $\min(0.6, 0.7) = 0.6$
- $\mu_{R \circ S}(x_1, z_1) = \max(0.4, 0.6) = 0.6$

**Entry $(x_1, z_2)$:**
- via $y_1$: $\min(0.4, 0.2) = 0.2$
- via $y_2$: $\min(0.6, 0.3) = 0.3$
- $\mu_{R \circ S}(x_1, z_2) = \max(0.2, 0.3) = 0.3$

**Entry $(x_2, z_1)$:**
- via $y_1$: $\min(0.1, 0.5) = 0.1$
- via $y_2$: $\min(0.9, 0.7) = 0.7$
- $\mu_{R \circ S}(x_2, z_1) = \max(0.1, 0.7) = 0.7$

**Entry $(x_2, z_2)$:**
- via $y_1$: $\min(0.1, 0.2) = 0.1$
- via $y_2$: $\min(0.9, 0.3) = 0.3$
- $\mu_{R \circ S}(x_2, z_2) = \max(0.1, 0.3) = 0.3$

**Step 3 — Final Result:**

$$R \circ S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 \\ 0.7 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Mark distribution: 1 mark for setup, 4 marks (1 per entry), 2 marks for correct final matrix.*

---

### Question B (Alternative) — 14 Marks

**Question B (14 Marks) — Equivalence Relation and Projection**

**(a)** Define a fuzzy equivalence relation. Verify whether the following fuzzy relation on $X = \{a, b, c\}$ is a fuzzy equivalence relation: **(7 marks)**

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.6 & 1.0 & 0.8 \\ 0.4 & 0.8 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(b)** For the relation $A \times B$ obtained in the previous part (a) of this question's setup, find the projection onto $X$ and onto $Y$, and the cylindrical extension of $A$ to $X \times Y$ where $A = \{(a, 0.5),\; (b, 0.7)\}$ and $Y = \{y_1, y_2, y_3\}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (1 mark):** A fuzzy equivalence relation is a fuzzy relation on $X \times X$ that is **reflexive**, **symmetric**, and **transitive** (the latter in the max–min sense).

**Reflexivity check (1 mark):** $\mu_R(a, a) = 1$, $\mu_R(b, b) = 1$, $\mu_R(c, c) = 1$. ✓ Reflexive.

**Symmetry check (2 marks):** $R$ is symmetric across the main diagonal: $\mu_R(a, b) = \mu_R(b, a) = 0.6$, $\mu_R(a, c) = \mu_R(c, a) = 0.4$, $\mu_R(b, c) = \mu_R(c, b) = 0.8$. ✓ Symmetric.

**Transitivity check (3 marks):** For each pair $(x, z)$, verify $\mu_R(x, z) \geq \max_y \min(\mu_R(x, y), \mu_R(y, z))$.

Take $(a, c)$:
- via $b$: $\min(0.6, 0.8) = 0.6$
- max over $y$ = $0.6$
- $\mu_R(a, c) = 0.4$, and $0.4 \not\geq 0.6$. ✗

Take $(a, b)$:
- via $c$: $\min(0.4, 0.8) = 0.4$
- via $b$ (not allowed in composition, but consider $y = a$ and $y = c$): max = 0.4
- $\mu_R(a, b) = 0.6 \geq 0.4$. ✓

The transitivity condition fails for $(a, c)$. Hence $R$ is **not a fuzzy equivalence relation**.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Form $A \times B$ (with $B$ taken from Question A part a) for illustration, but as the problem redefines $A$, we form a fresh product using the $A$ given here.** Let $B$ be the fuzzy set $\{(y_1, 0.5), (y_2, 0.6), (y_3, 0.2)\}$ (assumed from context).

Cartesian product using min:
$$A \times B = \begin{bmatrix} \min(0.5, 0.5) & \min(0.5, 0.6) & \min(0.5, 0.2) \\ \min(0.7, 0.5) & \min(0.7, 0.6) & \min(0.7, 0.2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.5 & 0.6 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Projection onto $X$ (row-wise max, 2 marks):**
- $(A \times B)_X(a) = \max(0.5, 0.5, 0.2) = 0.5$
- $(A \times B)_X(b) = \max(0.5, 0.6, 0.2) = 0.6$

So projection onto $X$ = $\{(a, 0.5),\; (b, 0.6)\}$. *(Observe: this is not the same as original $A$ because $B$'s maximum is 0.6, the limiting factor.)*

**Step 3 — Projection onto $Y$ (column-wise max, 2 marks):**
- $(A \times B)_Y(y_1) = \max(0.5, 0.5) = 0.5$
- $(A \times B)_Y(y_2) = \max(0.5, 0.6) = 0.6$
- $(A \times B)_Y(y_3) = \max(0.2, 0.2) = 0.2$

Projection onto $Y$ = $\{(y_1, 0.5),\; (y_2, 0.6),\; (y_3, 0.2)\} = B$. ✓ Recovers $B$ exactly.

**Step 4 — Cylindrical extension of $A$ to $X \times Y$ (1 mark):** Replicate $\mu_A(x)$ across all columns:

$$\bar{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.7 & 0.7 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Mark distribution: 1 mark setup, 2 marks projection on $X$, 2 marks projection on $Y$, 1 mark cylindrical extension, 1 mark final compilation.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Order of operations in composition:** For max–min, students often confuse and use max on the *inside* and min on the *outside* — this is worth 0.
> 2. **Index alignment:** The row of $R$ and column of $S$ must share the same intermediate variable $y$. Mixing up $y_1$ and $y_2$ leads to wrong entries that are *plausible* (not zero), so the examiner deducts full marks for the row/column.
> 3. **Transitivity check:** Many students only test reflexive + symmetric and stop. **Always** check the max–min transitivity inequality for at least one non-diagonal pair to confirm equivalence.
> 4. **Unit interval check:** Forgetting to verify all entries lie in $[0, 1]$ may cost 0.5–1 mark in computations involving max–product.
> 5. **Cartesian product operator:** Writing $A \cdot B$ (multiplication) when the question demands min, or vice-versa, is a very common 1–2 mark error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Fuzzy Relations**
>
> - **Fuzzy relation** = fuzzy subset of a Cartesian product, with $\mu_R : X \times Y \rightarrow [0, 1]$.
> - **Operations** are entry-wise: union $\to$ max, intersection $\to$ min, complement $\to$ $1 - \mu$.
> - **Cartesian product** of two fuzzy sets $A$ (on $X$) and $B$ (on $Y$): $\mu_{A \times B}(x, y) = \min(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(y))$ — KTU default.
> - **Max–min composition** $R \circ S$: $\mu_{R \circ S}(x, z) = \max_y \min(\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(y, z))$ — *most-tested* operation.
> - **Max–product composition**: same skeleton, replace min with multiplication.
> - **Projection** onto a factor space = row-wise or column-wise max.
> - **Cylindrical extension** of a fuzzy set $A$ = replicate its membership across the extra dimension.
> - **Fuzzy equivalence relation** = reflexive + symmetric + (max–min) transitive.
> - Membership values must always lie in the closed unit interval $[0, 1]$.
> - A fuzzy equivalence relation induces a **fuzzy partition**, foundational for fuzzy clustering.
> - In max–min composition, **always** check the intermediate index set $Y$ is non-empty; otherwise the max is undefined.
> - The composition $R \circ S$ is well-defined only when the *column count* of $R$ equals the *row count* of $S$ (matrix compatibility).
> - Common KTU traps: confusing min and max, swapping the order of $R$ and $S$, ignoring transitivity when checking equivalence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations on Fuzzy Relations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT521 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operations on Fuzzy Relations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Fuzzy Relation

Let **X** and **Y** be two crisp (classical) sets. A **binary fuzzy relation R** between X and Y is a fuzzy subset of the Cartesian product **X × Y**, formally written as:

$$R = \{(x, y), \mu_R(x, y) \vert (x, y) \in X \times Y\}$$

where the **membership function** $\mu_R : X \times Y \rightarrow [0, 1]$ assigns to every ordered pair $(x, y)$ a degree of relationship lying in the closed unit interval **[0, 1]**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization of Crisp Relations:** A crisp relation is simply a fuzzy relation whose membership values are restricted to the set **{0, 1}**. Fuzzy relations therefore extend boolean "either/or" linkages into a graded "how much" continuum.

When **X = Y**, R is called a **fuzzy relation on a single set**; when X ≠ Y, R is a **fuzzy relation between two sets**. For finite X and Y of cardinalities m and n, the fuzzy relation is conveniently represented as an **m × n fuzzy matrix** whose entries are values in [0, 1].

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the relationship *x is approximately equal to y* between real numbers. Two values such as 7 and 7.05 do not satisfy the relation absolutely, yet neither are they completely unrelated — they are "almost" related. A fuzzy relation captures precisely this **graded affinity** that classical crisp sets cannot express.

Another everyday analogy is **the similarity of two students' handwriting styles**:

- Crisp relation says: *"either similar (1) or not (0)"* — too coarse.
- Fuzzy relation says: *"75 % similar, 40 % similar, 90 % similar…"* — capturing the human intuition of partial resemblance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are Fuzzy Relations important in Engineering?** They form the backbone of fuzzy expert systems, fuzzy control (Mamdani and Takagi-Sugeno architectures), fuzzy database retrieval, fuzzy clustering, medical diagnosis (symptom-to-disease mapping), and decision-support systems. Almost every soft-computing application that uses the if-then rule base relies on fuzzy relational operations.

### 1.3 Empty, Universal, and Identity Relations

- **Empty relation** R = ∅ : all memberships are **0**.
- **Universal relation** R = X × Y : all memberships are **1**.
- **Identity relation** I on X: $\mu_I(x, y) = 1$ if $x = y$, else **0** — analogous to the identity matrix in linear algebra.
- **Diagonal relation** D ⊆ I: contains only the diagonal pairs of X.

### 1.4 Notation Conventions Used Across KTU 2024 Scheme

- **R ∪ S** — fuzzy union (logical OR)
- **R ∩ S** — fuzzy intersection (logical AND)
- **R̄** or **Rᶜ** — fuzzy complement (logical NOT)
- **R ∘ S** — fuzzy composition
- **R⁻¹** — inverse (converse) relation obtained by transposing the matrix

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU exam trick is to confuse **complement** with **inverse relation**. Remember: *complement* changes membership values via 1 − μ, while *inverse* only swaps the order of the pair (and is equivalent to matrix transposition).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Set-Theoretic Operations

Let R and S be two fuzzy relations defined on the same universe X × Y. The pointwise membership values are computed for every ordered pair $(x, y) \in X \times Y$.

#### 2.1.1 Fuzzy Union (R ∪ S)

- **Intuition:** Captures the **logical OR** — the pair belongs to the combined relation if it belongs to *either* R *or* S, taking the stronger of the two memberships.
- **Logic steps:**
  1. For each pair $(x, y)$, look up the membership in R and in S.
  2. Pick the larger of the two values (this is the **s-norm / t-conorm maximum**).
  3. The result is again a fuzzy relation in [0, 1].

#### 2.1.2 Fuzzy Intersection (R ∩ S)

- **Intuition:** Captures the **logical AND** — the pair belongs to the joint relation only when it belongs to *both* R and S, taking the weaker of the two memberships.
- **Logic steps:**
  1. Read $\mu_R(x, y)$ and $\mu_S(x, y)$.
  2. Pick the smaller value (**t-norm / minimum**).
  3. Output is a fuzzy relation.

#### 2.1.3 Fuzzy Complement (R̄)

- **Intuition:** Captures the **logical NOT** — grades of belonging are inverted so that strongly-related pairs become weakly-unrelated.
- **Logic steps:**
  1. Subtract each membership from **1**.
  2. Universal relation becomes empty, and vice versa.

### 2.2 Fuzzy Composition (The Most Marked Operation in KTU Exams)

Composition is the single most exam-relevant operation because it underlies the **fuzzy inference engine**. Given R from X to Y and S from Y to Z, the **max-min composition** produces a relation R ∘ S from X to Z.

#### 2.2.1 Max–Min Composition

- **Why "max-min"?** We sweep over every possible intermediate $y \in Y$, combine R(x, y) and S(y, z) via the **minimum** (weakest link in the chain), and then take the **maximum** over all intermediate y's (strongest such chain).
- **Logic steps:**
  1. For each x ∈ X and z ∈ Z:
  2. For every intermediate y ∈ Y, compute $\min[\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(y, z)]$.
  3. Retain the largest of these minima.

#### 2.2.2 Max–Product Composition

- Instead of using minimum to combine R(x, y) and S(y, z), we use the **algebraic product**.
- Useful when membership values are interpreted as probabilities of independent events.

#### 2.2.3 Max–Average Composition

- Combines the two relations via their arithmetic average before taking the maximum.

### 2.3 Projection and Cylindrical Extension

#### 2.3.1 Projection (marginalization)

Drops one of the universes and collapses the membership by taking the supremum over the dropped dimension.

#### 2.3.2 Cylindrical Extension

The reverse of projection: embeds a fuzzy set from a lower-dimensional space back into the higher-dimensional product space, with no loss of information.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Operation | Symbol | Membership Formula (pointwise) | Engineering Analogy |
|---|-----------|--------|--------------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Union | $R \cup S$ | $\mu_{R \cup S}(x, y) = \max[\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(x, y)]$ | OR-gate (parallel reliability) |
| 2 | Intersection | $R \cap S$ | $\mu_{R \cap S}(x, y) = \min[\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(x, y)]$ | AND-gate (series reliability) |
| 3 | Complement | $\overline{R}$ | $\mu_{\overline{R}}(x, y) = 1 - \mu_R(x, y)$ | NOT-gate (inverter) |
| 4 | Inverse (Converse) | $R^{-1}$ | $\mu_{R^{-1}}(y, x) = \mu_R(x, y)$ | Matrix transpose |
| 5 | Max–Min Composition | $R \circ S$ | $\mu_{R \circ S}(x, z) = \max_{y} \min[\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(y, z)]$ | Chain of fuzzy if-then rules |
| 6 | Max–Product Composition | $R \circ S$ | $\mu_{R \circ S}(x, z) = \max_{y} [\mu_R(x, y) \cdot \mu_S(y, z)]$ | Stochastic chain rule |
| 7 | Max–Average Composition | $R \circ S$ | $\mu_{R \circ S}(x, z) = \max_{y} \tfrac{1}{2}[\mu_R(x, y) + \mu_S(y, z)]$ | Average-strength link |
| 8 | Projection onto X | $\text{proj}_X(R)$ | $\mu_{\text{proj}_X}(x) = \max_{y} \mu_R(x, y)$ | Marginal probability |
| 9 | Projection onto Y | $\text{proj}_Y(R)$ | $\mu_{\text{proj}_Y}(y) = \max_{x} \mu_R(x, y)$ | Marginal probability |
| 10 | Cylindrical Extension | $\text{ce}(A)$ | $\mu_{\text{ce}(A)}(x, y) = \mu_A(x)$ | Padding / lifting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Memorize for KTU:**
> - $\mu(\cdot) \in [0, 1]$ for every operation.
> - $R \cup \emptyset = R$, $R \cap X \times Y = R$, $\overline{\overline{R}} = R$.
> - Composition is **not commutative** in general: $R \circ S \neq S \circ R$.
> - Composition is **associative**: $(R \circ S) \circ T = R \circ (S \circ T)$.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Fuzzy controllers (washing machines, AC, ABS):** use **max–min composition** to chain sensor rules to actuator commands.
- **Medical diagnosis:** symptoms → diseases mapping composed with diseases → treatments mapping yields symptoms → treatments recommendations.
- **Recommender systems:** user-product likes composed with product-user friend network gives friend-recommended products.
- **Database querying:** *fuzzy SQL* uses projection and cylindrical extension to handle imprecise queries such as *"young employees earning high salary"*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Pointwise Set Operations

Let $X = \{x_1, x_2, x_3\}$ and $Y = \{y_1, y_2\}$. Define two fuzzy relations:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.7 \\ 0.9 & 0.4 \\ 0.5 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.9 \\ 0.7 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Each row corresponds to an element of X, each column to an element of Y.

**Step 1 — Compute the union $R \cup S$ (pointwise maximum):**

$$
R \cup S = \begin{bmatrix} \max(0.2, 0.6) & \max(0.7, 0.3) \\ \max(0.9, 0.4) & \max(0.4, 0.9) \\ \max(0.5, 0.7) & \max(0.8, 0.1) \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R \cup S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.7 \\ 0.9 & 0.9 \\ 0.7 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the intersection $R \cap S$ (pointwise minimum):**

$$
R \cap S = \begin{bmatrix} \min(0.2, 0.6) & \min(0.7, 0.3) \\ \min(0.9, 0.4) & \min(0.4, 0.9) \\ \min(0.5, 0.7) & \min(0.8, 0.1) \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R \cap S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.4 \\ 0.5 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the complement of R:**

$$
\overline{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 0.2 & 1 - 0.7 \\ 1 - 0.9 & 1 - 0.4 \\ 1 - 0.5 & 1 - 0.8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.3 \\ 0.1 & 0.6 \\ 0.5 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the inverse of R (transpose of matrix):**

$$
R^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.9 & 0.5 \\ 0.7 & 0.4 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Max–Min Composition (Exam Favourite)

Let $X = \{x_1, x_2\}$, $Y = \{y_1, y_2, y_3\}$, $Z = \{z_1, z_2\}$.

$$
R (X \times Y) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.7 & 0.2 \\ 0.8 & 0.5 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
S (Y \times Z) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.9 \\ 0.4 & 0.2 \\ 0.7 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute the max-min composition $T = R \circ S$, which will be a $2 \times 2$ relation on $X \times Z$.

**Step 1 — Compute entry $T(x_1, z_1)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x_1, z_1) &= \max_{y \in Y} \min[\mu_R(x_1, y), \mu_S(y, z_1)] \\
&= \max\{\min(\mu_R(x_1, y_1), \mu_S(y_1, z_1)), \min(\mu_R(x_1, y_2), \mu_S(y_2, z_1)), \min(\mu_R(x_1, y_3), \mu_S(y_3, z_1))\} \\
&= \max\{\min(0.3, 0.6), \min(0.7, 0.4), \min(0.2, 0.7)\} \\
&= \max\{0.3, 0.4, 0.2\} \\
&= 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute entry $T(x_1, z_2)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x_1, z_2) &= \max_{y \in Y} \min[\mu_R(x_1, y), \mu_S(y, z_2)] \\
&= \max\{\min(0.3, 0.9), \min(0.7, 0.2), \min(0.2, 0.5)\} \\
&= \max\{0.3, 0.2, 0.2\} \\
&= 0.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute entry $T(x_2, z_1)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x_2, z_1) &= \max_{y \in Y} \min[\mu_R(x_2, y), \mu_S(y, z_1)] \\
&= \max\{\min(0.8, 0.6), \min(0.5, 0.4), \min(0.6, 0.7)\} \\
&= \max\{0.6, 0.4, 0.6\} \\
&= 0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute entry $T(x_2, z_2)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x_2, z_2) &= \max_{y \in Y} \min[\mu_R(x_2, y), \mu_S(y, z_2)] \\
&= \max\{\min(0.8, 0.9), \min(0.5, 0.2), \min(0.6, 0.5)\} \\
&= \max\{0.8, 0.2, 0.5\} \\
&= 0.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the composed matrix T:**

$$
T = R \circ S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.3 \\ 0.6 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Max–Product Composition

Using the same R and S as above:

**Entry $T(x_1, z_1)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x_1, z_1) &= \max_{y} [\mu_R(x_1, y) \cdot \mu_S(y, z_1)] \\
&= \max\{(0.3 \times 0.6), (0.7 \times 0.4), (0.2 \times 0.7)\} \\
&= \max\{0.18, 0.28, 0.14\} \\
&= 0.28
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry $T(x_1, z_2)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x_1, z_2) &= \max_{y} [\mu_R(x_1, y) \cdot \mu_S(y, z_2)] \\
&= \max\{(0.3 \times 0.9), (0.7 \times 0.2), (0.2 \times 0.5)\} \\
&= \max\{0.27, 0.14, 0.10\} \\
&= 0.27
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry $T(x_2, z_1)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x_2, z_1) &= \max_{y} [\mu_R(x_2, y) \cdot \mu_S(y, z_1)] \\
&= \max\{(0.8 \times 0.6), (0.5 \times 0.4), (0.6 \times 0.7)\} \\
&= \max\{0.48, 0.20, 0.42\} \\
&= 0.48
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry $T(x_2, z_2)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x_2, z_2) &= \max_{y} [\mu_R(x_2, y) \cdot \mu_S(y, z_2)] \\
&= \max\{(0.8 \times 0.9), (0.5 \times 0.2), (0.6 \times 0.5)\} \\
&= \max\{0.72, 0.10, 0.30\} \\
&= 0.72
\end{aligned}
$$

**Composed Matrix:**

$$
T = R \circ S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.28 & 0.27 \\ 0.48 & 0.72 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Projection and Cylindrical Extension

Given the relation R on $X \times Y$ from Example 1:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.7 \\ 0.9 & 0.4 \\ 0.5 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Projection onto X** (column-wise maximum, since rows index X):

$$
\text{proj}_X(R) = \begin{bmatrix} \max(0.2, 0.7) \\ \max(0.9, 0.4) \\ \max(0.5, 0.8) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 \\ 0.9 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Projection onto Y** (row-wise maximum, since columns index Y):

$$
\text{proj}_Y(R) = \begin{bmatrix} \max(0.2, 0.9, 0.5) \\ \max(0.7, 0.4, 0.8) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Cylindrical Extension of** $\text{proj}_X(R)$ back to $X \times Y$:

$$
\text{ce}[\text{proj}_X(R)] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.7 \\ 0.9 & 0.9 \\ 0.8 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Each row of $\text{proj}_X(R)$ is replicated across every column of Y to embed the 1-D fuzzy set into 2-D space.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Operations on Fuzzy Relations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FUZZY RELATIONS ON X TIMES Y"] --> B["POINTWISE SET OPS"]
    A --> C["STRUCTURAL OPS"]
    A --> D["COMPOSITIONAL OPS"]

    B --> B1["UNION MAX MU"]
    B --> B2["INTERSECTION MIN MU"]
    B --> B3["COMPLEMENT ONE MINUS MU"]

    C --> C1["INVERSE TRANSPOSE"]
    C --> C2["PROJECTION SUPREMUM"]
    C --> C3["CYLINDRIC EXTENSION LIFTING"]

    D --> D1["MAX MIN COMPOSITION"]
    D --> D2["MAX PRODUCT COMPOSITION"]
    D --> D3["MAX AVERAGE COMPOSITION"]

    B1 --> R1["OUTPUT FUZZY MATRIX"]
    B2 --> R1
    B3 --> R1
    C1 --> R1
    C2 --> R1
    C3 --> R1
    D1 --> R1
    D2 --> R1
    D3 --> R1
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Max–Min Composition Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        IM["R MATRIX X BY Y"]
        IN["S MATRIX Y BY Z"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["COMPOSITION ENGINE R CIRCLE S"]
        P1["PICK ROW I OF R"]
        P2["PICK COLUMN K OF S"]
        P3["FOR EACH Y INDEX J COMPUTE MIN R I J AND S J K"]
        P4["COLLECT ALL MIN VALUES"]
        P5["TAKE MAX OVER COLUMN J"]
        P6["STORE AS T I K"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        OUT["T MATRIX X BY Z"]
    end

    IM --> P1
    IN --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Selecting the Correct Composition Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["START CHOOSE COMPOSITION"] --> Q1{"INTERPRETATION OF MEMBERSHIP"}
    Q1 -- DEGREE OF TRUTH STRENGTH --> Q2["USE MAX MIN"]
    Q1 -- PROBABILITY OF INDEPENDENT EVENTS --> Q3["USE MAX PRODUCT"]
    Q1 -- AVERAGE STRENGTH OF CHAIN --> Q4["USE MAX AVERAGE"]
    Q2 --> FIN["OUTPUT R CIRCLE S"]
    Q3 --> FIN
    Q4 --> FIN
```

> [!TIP]
> Always match the composition type to the **semantic meaning** of the membership values in your problem statement. KTU examiners love setting a question where students blindly apply max-min to a probability interpretation and lose half the marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Define a fuzzy relation. How does it differ from a crisp relation? Illustrate with an example involving the similarity between temperature readings.

**Model Answer:**

A fuzzy relation R between two non-empty sets X and Y is a fuzzy subset of the Cartesian product $X \times Y$, given by:

$$R = \{(x, y), \mu_R(x, y) \vert (x, y) \in X \times Y\}$$

where $\mu_R(x, y) \in [0, 1]$ denotes the degree of relationship between x and y. A crisp relation restricts $\mu_R$ to the binary set {0, 1}. Example: between temperature readings on two thermometers, the crisp relation "equal to within 1 °C" might give 1 only when $|T_1 - T_2| \leq 1$, while the fuzzy relation "approximately equal" could be defined as $\mu_R(T_1, T_2) = \max\{0, 1 - \vert T_1 - T_2 \vert / 5\}$, giving graded values such as 0.9, 0.6, 0.4.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** State and explain the max–min composition of two fuzzy relations with an appropriate formula.

**Model Answer:**

If R is a fuzzy relation from X to Y and S is a fuzzy relation from Y to Z, then the max–min composition $T = R \circ S$ is a fuzzy relation from X to Z defined by:

$$\mu_T(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} \min[\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(y, z)]$$

For each pair (x, z), the membership is computed by iterating over every intermediate element y, taking the **minimum** of the memberships along the two-step chain through y (weakest link), and then taking the **maximum** over all such chains (best path). It implements the **composition of fuzzy implications** and is widely used in fuzzy inference engines.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A — Operations Suite **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

Let $X = \{a, b, c\}$ and $Y = \{1, 2\}$. Two fuzzy relations are:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.9 \\ 0.7 & 0.2 \\ 0.5 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 \\ 0.8 & 0.5 \\ 0.1 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(a)** Compute $R \cup S$, $R \cap S$, $\overline{R}$, and the projection of R onto Y. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Define a third relation T from Y to Z where $Z = \{\alpha, \beta\}$ as:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.7 \\ 0.9 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Evaluate the max–min composition $S \circ T$. **[7 marks]**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — [7 marks]**

**Union (max):**

$$
R \cup S = \begin{bmatrix} \max(0.4, 0.6) & \max(0.9, 0.3) \\ \max(0.7, 0.8) & \max(0.2, 0.5) \\ \max(0.5, 0.1) & \max(0.6, 0.9) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.9 \\ 0.8 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Intersection (min):**

$$
R \cap S = \begin{bmatrix} \min(0.4, 0.6) & \min(0.9, 0.3) \\ \min(0.7, 0.8) & \min(0.2, 0.5) \\ \min(0.5, 0.1) & \min(0.6, 0.9) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.3 \\ 0.7 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Complement of R:**

$$
\overline{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.1 \\ 0.3 & 0.8 \\ 0.5 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Projection of R onto Y (column-wise maximum over rows of X):**

$$
\text{proj}_Y(R) = \begin{bmatrix} \max(0.4, 0.7, 0.5) \\ \max(0.9, 0.2, 0.6) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 \\ 0.9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Max–min composition $S \circ T$ [7 marks]**

S is $3 \times 2$ (X → Y) and T is $2 \times 2$ (Y → Z), so $S \circ T$ is $3 \times 2$ (X → Z).

**Entry (a, α):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{S \circ T}(a, \alpha) &= \max_y \min[\mu_S(a, y), \mu_T(y, \alpha)] \\
&= \max\{\min(0.6, 0.5), \min(0.3, 0.9)\} \\
&= \max\{0.5, 0.3\} = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry (a, β):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{S \circ T}(a, \beta) &= \max_y \min[\mu_S(a, y), \mu_T(y, \beta)] \\
&= \max\{\min(0.6, 0.7), \min(0.3, 0.4)\} \\
&= \max\{0.6, 0.3\} = 0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry (b, α):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{S \circ T}(b, \alpha) &= \max_y \min[\mu_S(b, y), \mu_T(y, \alpha)] \\
&= \max\{\min(0.8, 0.5), \min(0.5, 0.9)\} \\
&= \max\{0.5, 0.5\} = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry (b, β):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{S \circ T}(b, \beta) &= \max_y \min[\mu_S(b, y), \mu_T(y, \beta)] \\
&= \max\{\min(0.8, 0.7), \min(0.5, 0.4)\} \\
&= \max\{0.7, 0.4\} = 0.7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry (c, α):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{S \circ T}(c, \alpha) &= \max_y \min[\mu_S(c, y), \mu_T(y, \alpha)] \\
&= \max\{\min(0.1, 0.5), \min(0.9, 0.9)\} \\
&= \max\{0.1, 0.9\} = 0.9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry (c, β):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{S \circ T}(c, \beta) &= \max_y \min[\mu_S(c, y), \mu_T(y, \beta)] \\
&= \max\{\min(0.1, 0.7), \min(0.9, 0.4)\} \\
&= \max\{0.1, 0.4\} = 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final composed matrix:**

$$
S \circ T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 \\ 0.5 & 0.7 \\ 0.9 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

#### QUESTION B — Max-Product Composition and Engineering Interpretation **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

Consider a fuzzy inference scenario where R models the membership of symptoms → diseases and S models the membership of diseases → treatments.

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.6 & 0.1 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.7 \\ 0.9 & 0.5 \\ 0.3 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(a)** Compute the max–product composition $R \circ S$ and interpret each entry as the recommended strength of treatment for a given symptom profile. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** If a third relation U models treatment → side-effects as $U = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$, compute the chain composition $(R \circ S) \circ U$ using max–min and state one real-world engineering scenario where this chained fuzzy inference is used. **[7 marks]**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Max–Product composition [7 marks]**

R is $2 \times 3$ (Symptoms × Diseases), S is $3 \times 2$ (Diseases × Treatments), so $R \circ S$ is $2 \times 2$.

**Entry (1, 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(R \circ S)_{11} &= \max_d [\mu_R(1, d) \cdot \mu_S(d, 1)] \\
&= \max\{(0.2 \times 0.4), (0.5 \times 0.9), (0.3 \times 0.3)\} \\
&= \max\{0.08, 0.45, 0.09\} = 0.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry (1, 2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(R \circ S)_{12} &= \max_d [\mu_R(1, d) \cdot \mu_S(d, 2)] \\
&= \max\{(0.2 \times 0.7), (0.5 \times 0.5), (0.3 \times 0.6)\} \\
&= \max\{0.14, 0.25, 0.18\} = 0.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry (2, 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(R \circ S)_{21} &= \max_d [\mu_R(2, d) \cdot \mu_S(d, 1)] \\
&= \max\{(0.6 \times 0.4), (0.1 \times 0.9), (0.8 \times 0.3)\} \\
&= \max\{0.24, 0.09, 0.24\} = 0.24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry (2, 2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(R \circ S)_{22} &= \max_d [\mu_R(2, d) \cdot \mu_S(d, 2)] \\
&= \max\{(0.6 \times 0.7), (0.1 \times 0.5), (0.8 \times 0.6)\} \\
&= \max\{0.42, 0.05, 0.48\} = 0.48
\end{aligned}
$$

**Composed matrix:**

$$
R \circ S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.45 & 0.25 \\ 0.24 & 0.48 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Interpretation:** Symptom profile 1 (row 1) most strongly recommends treatment 1 with strength 0.45; symptom profile 2 most strongly recommends treatment 2 with strength 0.48.

---

**Part (b) — Chained max–min composition $(R \circ S) \circ U$ [7 marks]**

First compute $T = R \circ S$ using max–min from part (a)'s matrices (recomputing for clarity):

**Max-min R ∘ S entries:**

- $T_{11} = \max\{\min(0.2, 0.4), \min(0.5, 0.9), \min(0.3, 0.3)\} = \max\{0.2, 0.5, 0.3\} = 0.5$
- $T_{12} = \max\{\min(0.2, 0.7), \min(0.5, 0.5), \min(0.3, 0.6)\} = \max\{0.2, 0.5, 0.3\} = 0.5$
- $T_{21} = \max\{\min(0.6, 0.4), \min(0.1, 0.9), \min(0.8, 0.3)\} = \max\{0.4, 0.1, 0.3\} = 0.4$
- $T_{22} = \max\{\min(0.6, 0.7), \min(0.1, 0.5), \min(0.8, 0.6)\} = \max\{0.6, 0.1, 0.6\} = 0.6$

So $T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$ (Symptoms × Treatments).

Now compute $T \circ U$ where $U = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$ (Treatments × Side Effects):

**Entry (1, 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(T \circ U)_{11} &= \max\{\min(0.5, 0.6), \min(0.5, 0.4)\} \\
&= \max\{0.5, 0.4\} = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry (1, 2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(T \circ U)_{12} &= \max\{\min(0.5, 0.3), \min(0.5, 0.8)\} \\
&= \max\{0.3, 0.5\} = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry (2, 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(T \circ U)_{21} &= \max\{\min(0.4, 0.6), \min(0.6, 0.4)\} \\
&= \max\{0.4, 0.4\} = 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry (2, 2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(T \circ U)_{22} &= \max\{\min(0.4, 0.3), \min(0.6, 0.8)\} \\
&= \max\{0.3, 0.6\} = 0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final chained composition:**

$$
(R \circ S) \circ U = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Real-world engineering scenario:** Chained fuzzy inference is the heart of **two-stage expert systems** in industrial process control — for example, *sensor reading → actuator command → predicted wear* in a CNC machine, or *patient symptoms → drug → side-effect risk* in clinical decision support. The associative property guarantees that the chain can be evaluated in any grouping order without changing the result, which is why such systems can be **modularized** into independent knowledge bases.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing rows and columns**: The intermediate set Y must have the *same cardinality* as both the column count of R and the row count of S. If dimensions do not match, composition is **undefined**.
> 2. **Confusing min with max**: In max-min composition, min is applied **inside** (per y) and max **outside** (across y's). Reversing them gives nonsense.
> 3. **Forgetting to enclose pairs in braces**: KTU valuation keys penalize missing curly braces around matrices and missing $\circ$ between R and S in compositions.
> 4. **Assuming commutativity**: $R \circ S \neq S \circ R$ in general. Always check order.
> 5. **Translating min-max to product-min incorrectly**: Max-product uses **multiplication**, not minimum, inside.
> 6. **Skipping unit tests**: When the final matrix contains an entry > 1 or < 0, you have made an arithmetic error — KTU examiners will not give partial credit for impossible fuzzy values.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **fuzzy relation** is a fuzzy subset of $X \times Y$ with $\mu_R(x, y) \in [0, 1]$.
- **Pointwise operations**: Union = max, Intersection = min, Complement = 1 − μ. These are the **t-conorm** and **t-norm** generalizations.
- **Inverse relation** is matrix **transposition**; it does **not** alter membership values, only their order.
- **Max–Min composition** $R \circ S$: for each (x, z), take min across intermediate y, then max across y's. Forms the basis of **fuzzy inference chaining**.
- **Max–Product composition** substitutes multiplication for the inner minimum; used when memberships represent independent probabilities.
- **Projection** collapses one dimension via the supremum (max) operator; **cylindrical extension** lifts a lower-dimensional fuzzy set back up by replicating values.
- Composition is **associative** but **not commutative**; it is the matrix analogue of relational join in databases.
- All output memberships must remain within the closed interval **[0, 1]** — this is the universal sanity check.
- Fuzzy relations power **fuzzy expert systems**, **Mamdani/Takagi-Sugeno controllers**, **fuzzy database querying**, and **medical decision support** — every soft-computing exam question on applications can be traced back to these operations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuzzy Membership Functions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT521 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuzzy Membership Functions

# Module 2 — Fuzzy Membership Functions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuzzy Membership Function (MF)**
> A **Fuzzy Membership Function** $\mu_A(x)$ is a mathematical function that defines the degree of membership of an element $x \in X$ in a fuzzy set $A$ on the universe of discourse $X$. It maps every element of the input space to a real value in the closed unit interval $[0, 1]$, where $0$ indicates *no membership* and $1$ indicates *full membership*.

$$
\mu_A : X \rightarrow [0, 1]
$$

Where:
- $X$ = **Universe of Discourse** (the universal set of all possible input values).
- $A$ = **Fuzzy Set** defined over $X$.
- $\mu_A(x)$ = **Membership Grade / Degree of Membership** of element $x$ in set $A$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Classical Sets**
> In *classical (crisp) sets*, the characteristic function assigns values in $\{0, 1\}$ — either an element belongs or it does not. In *fuzzy sets*, the membership function permits *graded* or *partial* belonging, gracefully handling the **vagueness**, **imprecision**, and **linguistic ambiguity** inherent in human reasoning.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are describing the height of a group of people using words like **"short"**, **"medium"**, and **"tall"**.

- In the **crisp world**, a person who is 175 cm tall is either *tall* (1) or *not tall* (0). There is no in-between.
- In the **fuzzy world**, the same person might be "0.7 tall" — meaning they are *fairly tall* but not *completely tall*. A person at 160 cm might be "0.3 tall" (slightly tall).

The **membership function** is the curve that decides these graded values. It tells us: *"How much does a given height belong to the concept 'tall'?"*

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Intuition**
> Think of the *cruise control* of a car. The membership function for the linguistic variable **"speed = high"** does not abruptly flip from 0 to 1. Instead, it *gradually increases* as speed climbs — modeling the human-like reasoning embedded in a fuzzy logic controller.

### 1.3 Physical / Mathematical Constants and Metrics

- **Range of Membership Value**: Strictly bounded as $0 \leq \mu_A(x) \leq 1$.
- **Universe of Discourse ($X$)**: Can be discrete (e.g., $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$) or continuous (e.g., $[0, 100]$).
- **Support of MF**: The region where $\mu_A(x) > 0$.
- **Core of MF**: The region where $\mu_A(x) = 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The 2024 Scheme places strong emphasis on the *geometric* and *analytical* representation of membership functions. Students are expected to sketch and mathematically formulate standard MFs (Triangular, Trapezoidal, Gaussian, Bell, and Sigmoidal).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Fuzzy Membership Function — Key Features

A fuzzy MF is characterized by four geometric regions, each holding board-exam significance:

| **Feature** | **Mathematical Condition** | **Intuitive Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| **Core** | $\{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) = 1\}$ | The region of *full* membership |
| **Support** | $\{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) > 0\}$ | The region of *non-zero* membership |
| **Boundary** | $\{x \in X \mid 0 < \mu_A(x) < 1\}$ | The region of *partial* membership |
| **Crossover Points** | $\{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) = 0.5\}$ | Points of *maximum fuzziness / uncertainty* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these features matter in KTU exams**: Questions on *"sketch and label the core, support, and boundary"* appear routinely (3-mark conceptual questions). Memorize the set-builder notation precisely.

### 2.2 Standard Types of Membership Functions (KTU High-Yield)

#### (A) Triangular Membership Function (`trimf`)
Defined by three parameters $a \leq b \leq c$, representing the left foot, peak, and right foot respectively.

$$
\mu(x; a, b, c) = \begin{cases} 0 & x \leq a \\ \dfrac{x - a}{b - a} & a \leq x \leq b \\ \dfrac{c - x}{c - b} & b \leq x \leq c \\ 0 & x \geq c \end{cases}
$$

- **Why use it?** Simplest, computationally cheapest. Best for *real-time* embedded fuzzy systems.
- **When to use?** When the linguistic concept rises and falls *linearly* (e.g., "comfortable temperature" around $22^\circ C$).

#### (B) Trapezoidal Membership Function (`trapmf`)
Defined by four parameters $a \leq b \leq c \leq d$, producing a flat-top region.

$$
\mu(x; a, b, c, d) = \begin{cases} 0 & x \leq a \\ \dfrac{x - a}{b - a} & a \leq x \leq b \\ 1 & b \leq x \leq c \\ \dfrac{d - x}{d - c} & c \leq x \leq d \\ 0 & x \geq d \end{cases}
$$

- **Why use it?** Represents concepts with a *plateau* of full membership (e.g., "acceptable voltage" — any value in the safe range is fully acceptable).

#### (C) Gaussian Membership Function (`gaussmf`)
Defined by two parameters: center $c$ and width $\sigma > 0$.

$$
\mu(x; c, \sigma) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x - c)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

- **Why use it?** Smooth, infinitely differentiable. Excellent for *noisy sensor data* and *machine learning fuzzy classifiers*.

#### (D) Generalized Bell Membership Function (`gbellmf`)
Defined by three parameters $a, b, c$ where $a > 0$ controls width and $b > 0$ controls slope.

$$
\mu(x; a, b, c) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left\vert \dfrac{x - c}{a} \right\vert^{2b}}
$$

#### (E) Sigmoidal Membership Function (`sigmf`)
Defined by parameters $a$ (slope) and $c$ (crossover point).

$$
\mu(x; a, c) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp\!\left(-a(x - c)\right)}
$$

- **Why use it?** Represents *open-ended* concepts (e.g., "very high", "very cold") that monotonically increase or decrease without a clear boundary.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **MF Type** | **Parameters** | **Mathematical Form** | **Best Used For** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Triangular | $a, b, c$ | $\max\!\left(0, \min\!\left(\tfrac{x-a}{b-a}, \tfrac{c-x}{c-b}\right)\right)$ | Simple symmetric concepts |
| Trapezoidal | $a, b, c, d$ | $\max\!\left(0, \min\!\left(\tfrac{x-a}{b-a}, 1, \tfrac{d-x}{d-c}\right)\right)$ | Concepts with plateau |
| Gaussian | $c, \sigma$ | $\exp\!\left(-\tfrac{(x-c)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Smooth, noisy data |
| Bell-shaped | $a, b, c$ | $1 \big/ \left(1 + \left\vert \tfrac{x-c}{a} \right\vert^{2b}\right)$ | Asymmetric fuzzy regions |
| Sigmoidal | $a, c$ | $1 \big/ \left(1 + e^{-a(x-c)}\right)$ | Open-ended / monotonic concepts |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility in Real Systems**
> - **Automotive**: Fuzzy MFs in *anti-lock braking systems* (ABS) — slip ratio MFs are typically trapezoidal.
> - **Consumer Electronics**: Washing machine controllers use triangular MFs for "dirt level".
> - **Aerospace**: NASA used Gaussian-like MFs in the *Space Shuttle* attitude controller.
> - **Medical AI**: Bell-shaped MFs model blood pressure zones ("low", "normal", "high").

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Evaluating a Triangular MF

**Problem Statement (KTU Practice Style):**
A fuzzy set $A$ = "comfortable temperature" is defined on $X = [0, 50]^\circ C$ using a triangular MF with parameters $(a, b, c) = (15, 25, 35)$. Compute the membership values $\mu_A(x)$ for $x = 10, 20, 25, 30, 40$.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation:**

$$
\mu_A(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & x \leq a \\ \dfrac{x - a}{b - a} & a \leq x \leq b \\ \dfrac{c - x}{c - b} & b \leq x \leq c \\ 0 & x \geq c \end{cases}
$$

Substitute $(a, b, c) = (15, 25, 35)$:

$$
\mu_A(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & x \leq 15 \\ \dfrac{x - 15}{25 - 15} = \dfrac{x - 15}{10} & 15 \leq x \leq 25 \\ \dfrac{35 - x}{35 - 25} = \dfrac{35 - x}{10} & 25 \leq x \leq 35 \\ 0 & x \geq 35 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at each query point:**

> **Case 1: $x = 10$**
> Since $10 \leq 15$, use the first branch.
> 
> $$
> \mu_A(10) = 0
> $$
> 
> *Interpretation*: $10^\circ C$ is *not comfortable* at all.

> **Case 2: $x = 20$**
> Since $15 \leq 20 \leq 25$, use the second branch.
> 
> $$
> \mu_A(20) = \dfrac{20 - 15}{10} = \dfrac{5}{10} = 0.5
> $$
> 
> *Interpretation*: $20^\circ C$ is *partially comfortable* (crossover point).

> **Case 3: $x = 25$**
> Since $25 \leq 25 \leq 35$, use the third branch.
> 
> $$
> \mu_A(25) = \dfrac{35 - 25}{10} = \dfrac{10}{10} = 1.0
> $$
> 
> *Interpretation*: $25^\circ C$ is *fully comfortable* (peak / core).

> **Case 4: $x = 30$**
> Since $25 \leq 30 \leq 35$, use the third branch.
> 
> $$
> \mu_A(30) = \dfrac{35 - 30}{10} = \dfrac{5}{10} = 0.5
> $$
> 
> *Interpretation*: $30^\circ C$ is *partially comfortable* (crossover point).

> **Case 5: $x = 40$**
> Since $40 \geq 35$, use the fourth branch.
> 
> $$
> \mu_A(40) = 0
> $$
> 
> *Interpretation*: $40^\circ C$ is *not comfortable* at all.

**Step 3 — Tabulated result:**

| Input $x$ ($^\circ C$) | Branch Used | $\mu_A(x)$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 1st | 0.0 | Not comfortable |
| 20 | 2nd | 0.5 | Partially comfortable |
| 25 | 3rd | 1.0 | Fully comfortable (Core) |
| 30 | 3rd | 0.5 | Partially comfortable |
| 40 | 4th | 0.0 | Not comfortable |

### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: Trapezoidal MF Crossover Points

**Problem:** For a trapezoidal MF with $(a, b, c, d) = (0, 2, 6, 8)$, find the support, core, and crossover points.

**Step 1 — Identify the regions directly from the formula:**

$$
\mu(x; 0, 2, 6, 8) = \begin{cases} 0 & x \leq 0 \\ \dfrac{x - 0}{2 - 0} = \dfrac{x}{2} & 0 \leq x \leq 2 \\ 1 & 2 \leq x \leq 6 \\ \dfrac{8 - x}{8 - 6} = \dfrac{8 - x}{2} & 6 \leq x \leq 8 \\ 0 & x \geq 8 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Core** ($\mu = 1$):

$$
\text{Core} = \{x \mid \mu_A(x) = 1\} = [2, 6]
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Support** ($\mu > 0$):

$$
\text{Support} = \{x \mid \mu_A(x) > 0\} = (0, 8)
$$

**Step 4 — Identify Crossover Points** ($\mu = 0.5$):

> Left crossover: Solve $\dfrac{x}{2} = 0.5 \Rightarrow x = 1.0$
> 
> Right crossover: Solve $\dfrac{8 - x}{2} = 0.5 \Rightarrow x = 7.0$

$$
\text{Crossover Points} = \{1.0, 7.0\}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough: Gaussian MF Point Evaluation

**Problem:** For $\mu(x; c, \sigma) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-c)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ with $c = 5, \sigma = 2$, compute $\mu(5), \mu(7), \mu(3), \mu(9)$.

**Step 1 — Substitute and simplify the expression:**

$$
\mu(x) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x - 5)^2}{2(2)^2}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x - 5)^2}{8}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at each point:**

> **At $x = 5$:** $\mu(5) = \exp(0) = 1.0$ *(Peak / Core)*
> 
> **At $x = 7$:** $\mu(7) = \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{(2)^2}{8}\right) = \exp(-0.5) \approx 0.6065$
> 
> **At $x = 3$:** $\mu(3) = \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{(-2)^2}{8}\right) = \exp(-0.5) \approx 0.6065$ *(Symmetry verified)*
> 
> **At $x = 9$:** $\mu(9) = \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{(4)^2}{8}\right) = \exp(-2) \approx 0.1353$

> [!TIP]
> **Symmetry Property of Gaussian MF**
> Gaussian MFs are *radially symmetric* about the center $c$. This property is frequently tested: *"Prove that the Gaussian MF with parameters $(c, \sigma)$ is symmetric about $x = c$."* The answer lies in the fact that $(x-c)^2 = (c-x)^2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Membership Function Selection in a Fuzzy Inference System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT VARIABLE X"] --> nodeB["UNIVERSE OF DISCOURSE U"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Linguistic Concept?"}
    nodeC -- Symmetric Peak --> nodeD["Triangular MF"]
    nodeC -- Plateau Region --> nodeE["Trapezoidal MF"]
    nodeC -- Smooth Continuous --> nodeF["Gaussian MF"]
    nodeC -- Asymmetric Slope --> nodeG["Bell-Shaped MF"]
    nodeC -- Open Ended Monotonic --> nodeH["Sigmoidal MF"]
    nodeD --> nodeI["Fuzzifier Output mu A of x"]
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Rule Base and Inference Engine"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Defuzzifier and Crisp Output"]
```

### 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram of MF Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Triangular MF trimf"] --> nodeB["Parameters a b c"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Linear Ascending and Descending"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Core is a Single Point"]

    nodeE["Trapezoidal MF trapmf"] --> nodeF["Parameters a b c d"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Linear Ascending Flat Top Linear Descending"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Core is an Interval"]

    nodeI["Gaussian MF gaussmf"] --> nodeJ["Parameters c sigma"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Exponential Bell Curve"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Infinitely Smooth Support"]

    nodeM["Sigmoidal MF sigmf"] --> nodeN["Parameters a c"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Logistic S Curve"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["No Closed Support Open Ended"]
```

### 4.3 Feature-to-MF Mapping Matrix (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Membership Function Requirement"] --> nodeB{"Need a Flat Plateau?"}
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC["Use Trapezoidal MF"]
    nodeB -- No --> nodeD{"Need Infinite Smoothness?"}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE["Use Gaussian MF"]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF{"Need Open Ended Boundary?"}
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG["Use Sigmoidal MF"]
    nodeF -- No --> nodeH{"Need Asymmetric Slope Control?"}
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeI["Use Bell Shaped MF"]
    nodeH -- No --> nodeJ["Use Triangular MF"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide**
> - The first flowchart maps how an *input variable* flows through MF selection during the **fuzzification** stage of a Fuzzy Inference System (FIS).
> - The second flowchart compares the *internal parameter structure* of the five standard MFs required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.
> - The third flowchart is a **decision tree** that engineers use to *pick the right MF* for a given control application — a frequent viva question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*
Define a fuzzy membership function. List any **four** standard types of membership functions used in fuzzy logic systems.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A fuzzy membership function $\mu_A(x)$ is a mathematical mapping that assigns to each element $x$ of the universe of discourse $X$ a membership grade in the interval $[0, 1]$, indicating the degree to which $x$ belongs to the fuzzy set $A$.

$$
\mu_A : X \rightarrow [0, 1]
$$

The four standard types are:
1. **Triangular Membership Function** — defined by parameters $(a, b, c)$.
2. **Trapezoidal Membership Function** — defined by parameters $(a, b, c, d)$.
3. **Gaussian Membership Function** — defined by parameters $(c, \sigma)$.
4. **Sigmoidal Membership Function** — defined by parameters $(a, c)$.

*(Bell-shaped is the fifth type — commonly accepted as a substitute for any one of the above.)*

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*
Differentiate between the **core**, **support**, and **boundary** of a fuzzy set. Use a labeled sketch in your explanation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Region | Definition | Set-Builder Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Core** | Region of *full* membership | $\{x \mid \mu_A(x) = 1\}$ |
| **Support** | Region of *non-zero* membership | $\{x \mid \mu_A(x) > 0\}$ |
| **Boundary** | Region of *partial* membership | $\{x \mid 0 < \mu_A(x) < 1\}$ |

> **Sketch Description (for 3 full marks, always include this):**
> Draw a triangular MF peaking at $x = b$ with feet at $x = a$ and $x = c$. Mark the *single point* $b$ as the **core**, the open interval $(a, c)$ as the **support**, and the regions $(a, b)$ and $(b, c)$ as the **boundary**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Define a fuzzy membership function. Explain the Triangular and Trapezoidal Membership Functions with neat labeled diagrams and mathematical formulations.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* Consider a fuzzy set $A$ = "high speed" defined on the universe of discourse $X = [0, 200]$ km/h using a trapezoidal membership function with parameters $(a, b, c, d) = (60, 90, 140, 170)$. Compute the membership values for $x = 40, 80, 100, 150, 180$. Identify the core and support of $A$.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Definition + Triangular & Trapezoidal MF Explanation [7 Marks]:**

A fuzzy membership function $\mu_A(x)$ assigns a membership grade in $[0, 1]$ to each $x \in X$, representing the degree of belonging to a fuzzy set $A$.

**Triangular MF (`trimf`):** Defined by parameters $(a, b, c)$ with $a \leq b \leq c$.

$$
\mu(x; a, b, c) = \begin{cases} 0 & x \leq a \\ \dfrac{x - a}{b - a} & a \leq x \leq b \\ \dfrac{c - x}{c - b} & b \leq x \leq c \\ 0 & x \geq c \end{cases}
$$

> *Diagram*: A triangle with vertices at $(a, 0)$, $(b, 1)$, and $(c, 0)$. The core is the *single point* $x = b$. The support is the open interval $(a, c)$. *(Sketch this on the answer sheet — 1 mark reserved.)*

**Trapezoidal MF (`trapmf`):** Defined by parameters $(a, b, c, d)$ with $a \leq b \leq c \leq d$.

$$
\mu(x; a, b, c, d) = \begin{cases} 0 & x \leq a \\ \dfrac{x - a}{b - a} & a \leq x \leq b \\ 1 & b \leq x \leq c \\ \dfrac{d - x}{d - c} & c \leq x \leq d \\ 0 & x \geq d \end{cases}
$$

> *Diagram*: A trapezoid with vertices at $(a, 0)$, $(b, 1)$, $(c, 1)$, $(d, 0)$. The core is the *interval* $[b, c]$. The support is $(a, d)$. *(Sketch this on the answer sheet — 1 mark reserved.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Evaluation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Substitute parameters into the trapezoidal formula:**

$$
\mu(x; 60, 90, 140, 170) = \begin{cases} 0 & x \leq 60 \\ \dfrac{x - 60}{90 - 60} = \dfrac{x - 60}{30} & 60 \leq x \leq 90 \\ 1 & 90 \leq x \leq 140 \\ \dfrac{170 - x}{170 - 140} = \dfrac{170 - x}{30} & 140 \leq x \leq 170 \\ 0 & x \geq 170 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each point:**

> **At $x = 40$:** $40 \leq 60 \Rightarrow \mu = 0$ *(not high)*
> 
> **At $x = 80$:** $60 \leq 80 \leq 90 \Rightarrow \mu = \tfrac{80-60}{30} = \tfrac{20}{30} \approx 0.667$ *(partially high)*
> 
> **At $x = 100$:** $90 \leq 100 \leq 140 \Rightarrow \mu = 1.0$ *(fully high — in the core)*
> 
> **At $x = 150$:** $140 \leq 150 \leq 170 \Rightarrow \mu = \tfrac{170-150}{30} = \tfrac{20}{30} \approx 0.667$ *(partially high)*
> 
> **At $x = 180$:** $180 \geq 170 \Rightarrow \mu = 0$ *(not high)*

**Step 3 — Tabulate and identify regions:**

| $x$ (km/h) | Branch | $\mu_A(x)$ | Region |
|---|---|---|---|
| 40 | 1st | 0.0 | Outside support |
| 80 | 2nd | 0.667 | Boundary (rising) |
| 100 | 3rd | 1.0 | Core |
| 150 | 4th | 0.667 | Boundary (falling) |
| 180 | 5th | 0.0 | Outside support |

**Step 4 — State Core and Support:**

- **Core** = $\{x \mid \mu_A(x) = 1\} = [90, 140]$
- **Support** = $\{x \mid \mu_A(x) > 0\} = (60, 170)$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Explain the **Gaussian** and **Generalized Bell** membership functions with mathematical formulations. State **two** advantages of Gaussian MFs over triangular MFs.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* A fuzzy set $B$ = "moderate temperature" is defined on $X = [0, 50]^\circ C$ using a Gaussian MF with center $c = 25$ and width $\sigma = 8$. Compute the membership values at $x = 17, 25, 33, 41$. Verify the symmetry property of the Gaussian MF.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Gaussian and Bell MF Explanation [7 Marks]:**

**Gaussian MF (`gaussmf`):** Defined by center $c$ and spread $\sigma > 0$.

$$
\mu(x; c, \sigma) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x - c)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

- The curve peaks at $x = c$ with $\mu = 1$.
- As $|x - c| \to \infty$, $\mu \to 0$ smoothly (but never exactly reaches 0).
- The curve is **symmetric** about $x = c$.

**Generalized Bell MF (`gbellmf`):** Defined by $a > 0$, $b > 0$, and center $c$.

$$
\mu(x; a, b, c) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left\vert \dfrac{x - c}{a} \right\vert^{2b}}
$$

- The parameter $a$ controls the **width** of the curve.
- The parameter $b$ controls the **slope** at the crossover points.
- Unlike Gaussian, the Bell MF can be **asymmetric** if extended (using two different $a$ values on each side).

**Two Advantages of Gaussian over Triangular:**

1. **Smoothness & Differentiability**: The Gaussian MF is infinitely differentiable, making it suitable for gradient-based optimization (e.g., ANFIS — Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference Systems). Triangular MFs have non-differentiable kinks at $x = a, b, c$.
2. **No Sharp Corners / Better Noise Tolerance**: Triangular MFs have abrupt slope changes, which can cause chattering in control outputs. Gaussian MFs produce *smoother control surfaces*, ideal for noisy sensor data.

---

**(b) Gaussian MF Numerical Evaluation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Substitute parameters:**

$$
\mu(x; 25, 8) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x - 25)^2}{2(8)^2}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x - 25)^2}{128}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each query point:**

> **At $x = 17$:** $\mu(17) = \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{(17-25)^2}{128}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{64}{128}\right) = \exp(-0.5) \approx 0.6065$
> 
> **At $x = 25$:** $\mu(25) = \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{(25-25)^2}{128}\right) = \exp(0) = 1.0$ *(Peak)*
> 
> **At $x = 33$:** $\mu(33) = \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{(33-25)^2}{128}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{64}{128}\right) = \exp(-0.5) \approx 0.6065$
> 
> **At $x = 41$:** $\mu(41) = \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{(41-25)^2}{128}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{256}{128}\right) = \exp(-2) \approx 0.1353$

**Step 3 — Verify Symmetry:**

Since $(x - c)^2 = (c - x)^2$ for any real $x$ and $c$:

$$
\mu(17) = \mu(33) = 0.6065
$$

> *Observation*: $17$ and $33$ are *equidistant* from $c = 25$ (both at a distance of $8$). The Gaussian MF assigns them **identical** membership values, confirming the **symmetry property** $\mu(c + \delta) = \mu(c - \delta)$ for any offset $\delta$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the "Sketch" Instruction**: A 7-mark question on MFs almost always includes the phrase *"with neat labeled diagram"*. Skipping the diagram typically costs **1 to 1.5 marks**. Even a rough sketch with axes labeled and vertices marked is sufficient.
> 2. **Mixing Up Core and Support**: The *core* requires $\mu = 1$ (use **closed** bracket). The *support* requires $\mu > 0$ (use **open** bracket). Writing them as identical intervals is a frequent mistake.
> 3. **Parameter Ordering Error**: For triangular MFs, students sometimes write the formula assuming $a < b < c$ strictly. If $b = a$ or $b = c$ (degenerate case), the MF becomes a *trapezoid*. Always state the ordering assumption clearly.
> 4. **Gaussian MF Support Misconception**: The Gaussian MF *never* reaches $\mu = 0$ — it only *asymptotically* approaches 0. Hence its support is technically the *entire real line* $(-\infty, \infty)$, not a finite interval. Examiners often test this subtlety.
> 5. **Sign Errors in Sigmoidal MF**: The sigmoidal MF is *increasing* if $a > 0$ and *decreasing* if $a < 0$. Confusing this is a common slip.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Fuzzy Membership Function** $\mu_A : X \rightarrow [0, 1]$ is the *fundamental building block* of fuzzy set theory — every fuzzy logic system revolves around it.
- ✅ The **core** is where $\mu = 1$; the **support** is where $\mu > 0$; the **boundary** is the intermediate partial-membership region.
- ✅ The **Triangular MF** uses 3 parameters $(a, b, c)$; the **Trapezoidal MF** uses 4 parameters $(a, b, c, d)$; the **Gaussian MF** uses 2 parameters $(c, \sigma)$; the **Bell MF** uses 3 parameters $(a, b, c)$; the **Sigmoidal MF** uses 2 parameters $(a, c)$.
- ✅ The **Gaussian MF** is *infinitely smooth, symmetric, and differentiable* — preferred for ANFIS and gradient-based training.
- ✅ The **Trapezoidal MF** is the *only* standard MF with a *plateau* (interval-valued core).
- ✅ The **Sigmoidal MF** is the *only* standard MF suited for *open-ended* monotonic concepts (e.g., "very high", "very cold").
- ✅ **Symmetry Property**: $\mu(c + \delta) = \mu(c - \delta)$ holds for Gaussian and Bell MFs centered at $c$.
- ✅ **Asymptotic Behavior**: Gaussian MFs $\mu(x) \to 0$ as $x \to \pm\infty$ but *never reach zero exactly*.
- ✅ **Engineering Relevance**: MFs are the *fuzzifier* in any Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) — applications span automotive (ABS), consumer electronics (washing machines, ACs), aerospace (NASA Shuttle), and medical AI.
- ✅ For a 14-mark KTU question on MFs, *always* present: **Definition → Formula → Diagram → Numerical Example → Identification of Core/Support**. This 5-part structure guarantees full marks.
- ✅ **Crossover points** ($\mu = 0.5$) are the *most uncertain* regions of an MF — frequently asked in viva voce.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuzzification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT521 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuzzification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Fuzzification — The Gateway to Fuzzy Inference

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEITT521 – Soft Computing**, **Fuzzification** is formally defined as the **first stage of a Fuzzy Inference System (FIS)**. It is the process of transforming a *crisp* (precise, well-defined, numerical) input value into a *fuzzy* value by determining its **degree of membership** across one or more predefined linguistic fuzzy sets defined over a **universe of discourse**.

Mathematically, given a crisp input $x_0 \in U$ (the universe of discourse), fuzzification computes a set of membership values:

$$F(x_0) = \{ \mu_{A_1}(x_0),\; \mu_{A_2}(x_0),\; \dots,\; \mu_{A_n}(x_0) \}$$

where each $A_i$ is a linguistic fuzzy set (e.g., "Low", "Medium", "High") and $\mu_{A_i}(x_0) \in [0, 1]$ is the corresponding **membership grade**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> *"Fuzzification – Membership functions – Fuzzification process – Defuzzification – Methods of defuzzification."*
> Fuzzification is the **opening gate** of the fuzzy pipeline: **Fuzzification → Knowledge Base / Rule Base → Inference Engine → Defuzzification**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you walk into a classroom and ask: *"How hot is the room?"* A classical (crisp) thermometer gives you exactly one number — say, **28.3 °C**. That is precise but loses human context. Now imagine a human instead answering: *"It's somewhat warm, a little uncomfortable, but not unbearable."* This is a **linguistic, fuzzy description**.

**Fuzzification is the translator between these two worlds.** It takes the crisp number `28.3 °C` and asks: *"How much does 28.3 °C belong to 'Cold'?"*, *"How much to 'Warm'?"*, *"How much to 'Hot'?"* The answers are membership grades like $\{0.0, 0.7, 0.3\}$.

| Domain | Representation Style | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Crisp (Classical)** | Single precise number | $T = 28.3\ ^\circ C$ |
| **Fuzzy (Linguistic)** | Set of membership degrees | $T = \{\text{Warm}/0.7,\ \text{Hot}/0.3\}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Fuzzification does **not** create uncertainty — the input $x_0$ is still perfectly known. It simply expresses that *one crisp value can belong partially to several overlapping linguistic categories* at the same time.

## 1.3 Why Fuzzification is Needed

A standard Boolean controller (on/off) is brittle when faced with real-world imprecision (sensor noise, linguistic rules, human expertise). Fuzzy systems — pioneered by **Lotfi A. Zadeh (1965)** — require fuzzification for three core reasons:

1. **Bridging numerical sensors and linguistic expert rules** — engineers say "if temperature is *high*, then fan speed is *fast*"; we must convert "28.3" to "high = 0.3".
2. **Handling overlapping categories** — 28 °C is *both* warm and slightly hot.
3. **Enabling smooth, continuous control** — small changes in input cause small changes in output, unlike Boolean step-functions.

Standard fuzzification metrics used as benchmarks in literature:
- **Universe of Discourse** ($U$) — the total range of input values.
- **Linguistic Variable** (e.g., `Temperature`) and its **Linguistic Values** (Cold, Warm, Hot).
- **Membership Grade Range:** strictly bounded in $[0, 1]$.
- **Core** — elements with $\mu(x) = 1$.
- **Support** — elements with $\mu(x) > 0$.
- **Boundary** — elements with $\mu(x) = 0.5$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Fuzzification

The fuzzification process, as prescribed in the KTU 2024 scheme, follows four rigorously defined stages:

1. **Identify the crisp input variables** — For a fuzzy controller, these are the measured sensor outputs (e.g., error $e$, change-in-error $\Delta e$, temperature $T$).
2. **Define the Universe of Discourse ($U$)** — Fix the numerical range for each input. Example: $U_T = [0, 50]\ ^\circ C$.
3. **Partition $U$ into linguistic fuzzy sets** — Decide on the number and shape of membership functions (MFs). Typical MFs: *Triangular, Trapezoidal, Gaussian, Generalized Bell, Sigmoid.*
4. **Compute the membership grade** of the crisp input $x_0$ in *each* fuzzy set. The result is a vector of degrees in $[0, 1]$.

## 2.2 Common Membership Functions (MFs)

### (a) Triangular Membership Function

Defined by three parameters $\{a, b, c\}$ with $a < b < c$:

$$
\mu_{tri}(x;\ a,b,c) =
\begin{cases}
0, & x \le a \\[4pt]
\dfrac{x - a}{b - a}, & a \le x \le b \\[8pt]
\dfrac{c - x}{c - b}, & b \le x \le c \\[4pt]
0, & x \ge c
\end{cases}
$$

### (b) Trapezoidal Membership Function

Defined by four parameters $\{a, b, c, d\}$ with $a < b \le c < d$:

$$
\mu_{trap}(x;\ a,b,c,d) =
\begin{cases}
0, & x \le a \text{ or } x \ge d \\[4pt]
\dfrac{x - a}{b - a}, & a \le x \le b \\[8pt]
1, & b \le x \le c \\[8pt]
\dfrac{d - x}{d - c}, & c \le x \le d
\end{cases}
$$

### (c) Gaussian Membership Function

Defined by centre $c$ and spread $\sigma > 0$:

$$\mu_{gauss}(x;\ c, \sigma) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x - c)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

### (d) Singleton Membership Function

Used when the input is already regarded as a single point with full membership in exactly one set:

$$\mu_{sing}(x;\ x_0) = \begin{cases} 1, & x = x_0 \\ 0, & x \ne x_0 \end{cases}$$

## 2.3 Methods / Types of Fuzzification

| # | Method | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Singleton Fuzzification** | Crisp $x_0$ maps to a fuzzy singleton with $\mu(x_0) = 1$ and 0 elsewhere. | When input is reliable/precise; computationally cheapest. |
| 2 | **Gaussian Fuzzification** | $x_0$ is converted to a Gaussian-shaped fuzzy set with $\mu(x_0) = 1$ and decaying tails. | When input has small measurement noise. |
| 3 | **Triangular / Trapezoidal Fuzzification** | Maps $x_0$ onto one or more overlapping MFs. | Most common in KTU textbook problems. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key fuzzification formulas at a glance — memorize this for Part A and Part B of the ESE.**

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| Triangular MF | $\mu_{tri}(x) = \max\!\left(0,\ \min\!\left(\dfrac{x-a}{b-a},\ \dfrac{c-x}{c-b}\right)\right)$ | $a < b < c$ |
| Trapezoidal MF | $\mu_{trap}(x) = \max\!\left(0,\ \min\!\left(\dfrac{x-a}{b-a},\ 1,\ \dfrac{d-x}{d-c}\right)\right)$ | $a < b \le c < d$ |
| Gaussian MF | $\mu_{gauss}(x) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-c)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\sigma > 0$ |
| Singleton MF | $\mu_{sing}(x_0) = 1,\ \mu_{sing}(x) = 0\ (x \ne x_0)$ | $\mu \in \{0, 1\}$ |
| Core of fuzzy set | $\{x \in U \mid \mu_A(x) = 1\}$ | Full membership points |
| Support of fuzzy set | $\{x \in U \mid \mu_A(x) > 0\}$ | Non-zero region |
| $\alpha$-cut ($\alpha \in [0,1]$) | $A_\alpha = \{x \in U \mid \mu_A(x) \ge \alpha\}$ | Used in defuzzification |
| Universe of Discourse | $U = [x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$ | Range of input variable |

> **Note on absolute value rendering:** In the table above, division bars inside `\dfrac{...}{...}` are used wherever a magnitude would normally appear. If you write a vertical absolute value, prefer `\vert x \vert` so that the markdown table parser never breaks.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Fuzzification is the entry point of every practical fuzzy application:
- **Automotive:** Automatic gear shifting (Honda, Nissan), anti-lock braking.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Washing machines, air-conditioners, vacuum cleaners (LG, Samsung).
- **Industrial Control:** Crane control, cement kiln temperature control, robotics.
- **Image Processing:** Edge detection, segmentation using fuzzy MFs.
- **Medical AI:** Diagnostic expert systems where symptoms map fuzzily to diseases.

In every case, real sensors deliver crisp values, but the **expert knowledge base uses linguistic IF-THEN rules**, so fuzzification is the indispensable translator.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Example — Triangular MF

> **Problem (Model KTU-style):** Consider a fuzzy variable `Temperature` defined over $U = [0, 50]\ ^\circ C$ with three linguistic sets:
> - **Cold** = Triangular MF with parameters $(a, b, c) = (0, 0, 20)$.
> - **Warm** = Triangular MF with parameters $(a, b, c) = (10, 25, 40)$.
> - **Hot**  = Triangular MF with parameters $(a, b, c) = (30, 50, 50)$.
>
> A crisp input $x_0 = 22\ ^\circ C$ is measured. Fuzzify $x_0$.

**Step 1 — Set up Cold MF (peak at 0, ends at 20):**

$$
\mu_{Cold}(x) =
\begin{cases}
0, & x \le 0 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{x - 0}{20 - 0} = \dfrac{x}{20}, & 0 \le x \le 20 \\[4pt]
0, & x \ge 20
\end{cases}
$$

For $x_0 = 22$: since $22 \ge 20$, $\mu_{Cold}(22) = 0$.

**Step 2 — Set up Warm MF (rises from 10, peaks at 25, falls to 40):**

$$
\mu_{Warm}(x) =
\begin{cases}
0, & x \le 10 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{x - 10}{25 - 10} = \dfrac{x - 10}{15}, & 10 \le x \le 25 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{40 - x}{40 - 25} = \dfrac{40 - x}{15}, & 25 \le x \le 40 \\[4pt]
0, & x \ge 40
\end{cases}
$$

For $x_0 = 22$, we are in the rising slope ($10 \le 22 \le 25$):

$$
\mu_{Warm}(22) = \dfrac{22 - 10}{25 - 10} = \dfrac{12}{15} = 0.800
$$

**Step 3 — Set up Hot MF (rises from 30, peaks at 50):**

$$
\mu_{Hot}(x) =
\begin{cases}
0, & x \le 30 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{x - 30}{50 - 30} = \dfrac{x - 30}{20}, & 30 \le x \le 50 \\[4pt]
0, & x \ge 50
\end{cases}
$$

For $x_0 = 22$, since $22 \le 30$, $\mu_{Hot}(22) = 0$.

**Step 4 — Assemble the fuzzified vector:**

$$
F(22) = \left\{\dfrac{0.0}{\text{Cold}},\ \dfrac{0.8}{\text{Warm}},\ \dfrac{0.0}{\text{Hot}}\right\}
$$

> **Interpretation:** 22 °C is interpreted as **80% Warm, 0% Cold, 0% Hot**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Example — Trapezoidal MF

> **Problem:** Fuzzify $x_0 = 35$ for the linguistic set **"Fast"** defined as a trapezoidal MF with $(a, b, c, d) = (20, 30, 40, 50)$ over $U = [0, 60]$.

**Step 1 — Identify region.** The crisp input $x_0 = 35$ lies in the *flat top* of the trapezoid because $b = 30 \le 35 \le 40 = c$.

**Step 2 — Apply the rule for the plateau region:**

$$
\mu_{Fast}(35) = 1.000
$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check.** Since $35$ is strictly inside $[30, 40]$, the membership is at its maximum, confirming the input is *fully Fast*.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Example — Gaussian MF

> **Problem:** A fuzzy set **"Normal"** is Gaussian with centre $c = 37\ ^\circ C$ and spread $\sigma = 5$. Fuzzify $x_0 = 39\ ^\circ C$.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the Gaussian formula:**

$$
\mu_{Normal}(39) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(39 - 37)^2}{2 \cdot 5^2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator and denominator:**

$$
(39 - 37)^2 = 2^2 = 4
$$

$$
2 \cdot 5^2 = 2 \cdot 25 = 50
$$

**Step 3 — Combine and evaluate:**

$$
\mu_{Normal}(39) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{4}{50}\right) = \exp(-0.08)
$$

$$
\mu_{Normal}(39) \approx 0.9231
$$

> **Interpretation:** 39 °C has a **92.31%** degree of membership in "Normal".

## 3.4 Exhaustive Numerical Example — Singleton Fuzzification

> **Problem:** A sensor reliably returns $x_0 = 5.0$ V. Fuzzify it using singleton MF centred at $5.0$.

**Step 1 — Apply the singleton definition:**

$$
\mu_{sing}(5.0) = 1, \qquad \mu_{sing}(x) = 0 \;\; \text{for all } x \ne 5.0
$$

**Step 2 — Consequence for the rule base:** Each rule whose antecedent mentions $x = 5.0$ fires with full strength; all other rules fire with zero strength. This is the **lightest computational cost** fuzzification scheme, and it is the KTU-recommended method when sensors are accurate.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Example | Input $x_0$ | MF Type | Parameters | $\mu(x_0)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ex. 1 | 22 (Cold) | Triangular | (0, 0, 20) | 0.000 |
| Ex. 1 | 22 (Warm) | Triangular | (10, 25, 40) | 0.800 |
| Ex. 1 | 22 (Hot)  | Triangular | (30, 50, 50) | 0.000 |
| Ex. 2 | 35 (Fast) | Trapezoidal | (20, 30, 40, 50) | 1.000 |
| Ex. 3 | 39 (Normal) | Gaussian | $c=37, \sigma=5$ | 0.9231 |
| Ex. 4 | 5.0 V (Input) | Singleton | $x_0 = 5.0$ | 1.000 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
