| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Bellman–Zadeh decision | $\mu_D(x) = \mu_G(x) \wedge \mu_C(x)$ | Min operator, most common |
| 2 | Multi-goal multi-constraint | $\mu_D(x) = \min_{i,j} \{\mu_{G_i}, \mu_{C_j}\}$ | Symmetric aggregation |
| 3 | Product operator | $\mu_D(x) = \mu_G(x) \cdot \mu_C(x)$ | Compensatory, less conservative |
| 4 | Weighted aggregation | $S_i = \sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k \cdot \mu_{ik}$ | MCDM with crisp/fuzzy weights |
| 5 | Optimal decision | $x^* = \arg\max_{x \in X} \mu_D(x)$ | Final defuzzified output |
| 6 | Centroid defuzzification | $x^* = \int x \mu(x) dx / \int \mu(x) dx$ | Most widely used |
| 7 | Yager's index | $Y(\tilde{A}) = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^1 (\underline{a}_\alpha + \bar{a}_\alpha) d\alpha$ | Ranking fuzzy numbers |
| 8 | α-cut of fuzzy set | $A_\alpha = \{x : \mu_A(x) \geq \alpha\}$ | Crisp set at level $\alpha$ |
| 9 | Triangular fuzzy number | $\tilde{A} = (a, b, c)$ with $\mu(x) = \max(0, \min(\frac{x-a}{b-a}, \frac{c-x}{c-b}))$ | Standard representation |
| 10 | Fuzzy linear programming | Optimize $\tilde{c}^T x$ s.t. $\tilde{A}x \leq \tilde{b}$, $x \geq 0$ | MODM formulation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Fuzzy decision making is extensively used in **medical diagnosis** (symptom-based disease identification), **financial portfolio selection** (risk-return tradeoff), **manufacturing** (process parameter optimization), **autonomous vehicles** (lane-change and obstacle-avoidance decisions), and **power systems** (load dispatch under uncertainty).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Bellman–Zadeh Decision (Min Operator)

**Problem:** A company wants to select the production level $x$ (in hundreds of units) for a new product. The fuzzy goal is to *maximize profit*, and the fuzzy constraint is to *stay within acceptable production capacity*. Both are defined over the discrete universe $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ as follows:

| $x$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mu_G(x)$ (Profit) | 0.1 | 0.4 | 0.7 | 1.0 | 0.8 | 0.5 |
| $\mu_C(x)$ (Capacity) | 0.6 | 0.9 | 1.0 | 0.9 | 0.6 | 0.2 |

**Find the optimal fuzzy decision $\mu_D(x)$ and the crisp optimal decision $x^*$.**

#### Step 1 — Identify the inputs

We have a universe $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$, a fuzzy goal $G$ with $\mu_G(x)$ values, and a fuzzy constraint $C$ with $\mu_C(x)$ values.

#### Step 2 — Apply the Bellman–Zadeh formula

Using the **minimum operator** (most common in KTU answers):

$$\mu_D(x) = \mu_G(x) \wedge \mu_C(x) = \min\{\mu_G(x), \mu_C(x)\}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute $\mu_D(x)$ for each $x$

- For $x = 1$: $\mu_D(1) = \min\{0.1, 0.6\} = 0.1$
- For $x = 2$: $\mu_D(2) = \min\{0.4, 0.9\} = 0.4$
- For $x = 3$: $\mu_D(3) = \min\{0.7, 1.0\} = 0.7$
- For $x = 4$: $\mu_D(4) = \min\{1.0, 0.9\} = 0.9$
- For $x = 5$: $\mu_D(5) = \min\{0.8, 0.6\} = 0.6$
- For $x = 6$: $\mu_D(6) = \min\{0.5, 0.2\} = 0.2$

#### Step 4 — Tabulate the fuzzy decision

| $x$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mu_D(x)$ | 0.1 | 0.4 | 0.7 | **0.9** | 0.6 | 0.2 |

#### Step 5 — Identify the optimal crisp decision

$$x^* = \arg\max_{x \in X} \mu_D(x) = 4$$

The maximum membership is $0.9$, attained at $x = 4$. Therefore, the company should produce **400 units**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Notice that $x=3$ achieves $\mu_G(3) = 0.7$ (good profit) but is limited by the lower $\mu_C(3) = 1.0$ (capacity allows it), so $\mu_D(3) = 0.7$. At $x=4$, both goal and constraint are well-satisfied, yielding the highest combined score. This demonstrates the **trade-off balancing** nature of fuzzy decision making.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Multi-Criteria Decision Making (MCDM)

**Problem:** An engineer must select the best location among three sites $A_1, A_2, A_3$ for a new factory, evaluated against three criteria: *Cost* (weight 0.4), *Accessibility* (weight 0.3), and *Environmental Impact* (weight 0.3). The membership grades are:

| Criterion | Weight $w_k$ | $A_1$ | $A_2$ | $A_3$ |
|-----------|--------------|-------|-------|-------|
| Cost | 0.4 | 0.8 | 0.6 | 0.9 |
| Accessibility | 0.3 | 0.7 | 0.9 | 0.5 |
| Environmental Impact | 0.3 | 0.6 | 0.8 | 0.7 |

**Determine the optimal site.**

#### Step 1 — Write the weighted aggregation formula

$$S_i = \sum_{k=1}^{3} w_k \cdot \mu_{ik}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute $S_1$ for site $A_1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= w_1 \cdot \mu_{11} + w_2 \cdot \mu_{12} + w_3 \cdot \mu_{13} \\
&= (0.4)(0.8) + (0.3)(0.7) + (0.3)(0.6) \\
&= 0.32 + 0.21 + 0.18 \\
&= 0.71
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute $S_2$ for site $A_2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_2 &= w_1 \cdot \mu_{21} + w_2 \cdot \mu_{22} + w_3 \cdot \mu_{23} \\
&= (0.4)(0.6) + (0.3)(0.9) + (0.3)(0.8) \\
&= 0.24 + 0.27 + 0.24 \\
&= 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute $S_3$ for site $A_3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_3 &= w_1 \cdot \mu_{31} + w_2 \cdot \mu_{32} + w_3 \cdot \mu_{33} \\
&= (0.4)(0.9) + (0.3)(0.5) + (0.3)(0.7) \\
&= 0.36 + 0.15 + 0.21 \\
&= 0.72
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Rank and select

| Site | $A_1$ | $A_2$ | $A_3$ |
|------|-------|-------|-------|
| Score $S_i$ | 0.71 | **0.75** | 0.72 |
| Rank | 3rd | **1st** | 2nd |

$$\boxed{A^* = A_2 \text{ (best site, with weighted score } 0.75)}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Centroid Defuzzification of a Triangular Fuzzy Number

**Problem:** Defuzzify the triangular fuzzy number $\tilde{A} = (2, 5, 8)$ to obtain a crisp value.

The triangular membership function is:

$$
\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 
\begin{cases}
\frac{x - 2}{5 - 2} = \frac{x-2}{3}, & 2 \leq x \leq 5 \\
\\
\frac{8 - x}{8 - 5} = \frac{8-x}{3}, & 5 \leq x \leq 8 \\
\\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Apply the centroid formula:**

$$x^* = \frac{\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x \cdot \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \, dx}{\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \, dx}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the denominator (area):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \, dx &= \int_{2}^{5} \frac{x-2}{3} \, dx + \int_{5}^{8} \frac{8-x}{3} \, dx \\
&= \frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{(x-2)^2}{2}\right]_{2}^{5} + \frac{1}{3}\left[-\frac{(8-x)^2}{2}\right]_{5}^{8} \\
&= \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{9}{2} + \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{9}{2} \\
&= \frac{3}{2} + \frac{3}{2} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator (first moment):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x \cdot \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \, dx &= \int_{2}^{5} x \cdot \frac{x-2}{3} \, dx + \int_{5}^{8} x \cdot \frac{8-x}{3} \, dx \\
&= \frac{1}{3} \int_{2}^{5} (x^2 - 2x) \, dx + \frac{1}{3} \int_{5}^{8} (8x - x^2) \, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

For the first integral:

$$
\frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{x^3}{3} - x^2\right]_{2}^{5} = \frac{1}{3}\left[\left(\frac{125}{3} - 25\right) - \left(\frac{8}{3} - 4\right)\right] = \frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{50}{3} - \frac{-4}{3}\right] = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{54}{3} = 6
$$

For the second integral:

$$
\frac{1}{3}\left[4x^2 - \frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{5}^{8} = \frac{1}{3}\left[\left(256 - \frac{512}{3}\right) - \left(100 - \frac{125}{3}\right)\right] = \frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{256}{3} - \frac{175}{3}\right] = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 27 = 9
$$

Total numerator = $6 + 9 = 15$.

**Step 3 — Compute the centroid:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^* &= \frac{15}{3} \\
&= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** For a symmetric triangular fuzzy number $(a, b, c)$ with $b - a = c - b$, the centroid is exactly the **peak** $b$. Here $5 - 2 = 8 - 5 = 3$ — perfectly symmetric — and the answer is exactly $b = 5$. ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operator Comparison

| Operator | $\mu_D(x) = \mu_G(x) \star \mu_C(x)$ | Compensatory? | Conservatism | KTU Preference |
|----------|---------------------------------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Min (Mamdani)** | $\min(\mu_G, \mu_C)$ | No | High | **Most common** |
| **Algebraic Product** | $\mu_G \cdot \mu_C$ | Yes | Medium | Sometimes asked |
| **Bounded Product** | $\max(0, \mu_G + \mu_C - 1)$ | No | Highest | Rare |
| **Drastic Product** | $\mu_G$ if $\mu_C = 1$ else $\mu_C$ if $\mu_G = 1$ else 0 | No | Extreme | Rare |
| **Hamacher (with $\gamma=2$)** | $\frac{\mu_G \cdot \mu_C}{2 - (\mu_G + \mu_C - \mu_G \mu_C)}$ | Yes | Adjustable | Advanced |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Pattern:** In most numerical problems, the **minimum (min) operator** is the default expectation. If the problem does not specify the operator, write the min version and **briefly mention** that the product operator is an alternative. This dual-mention strategy demonstrates mastery and earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Bellman–Zadeh Fuzzy Decision Making Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT FUZZY GOAL G] --> C[FUZZY DECISION SPACE]
    B[INPUT FUZZY CONSTRAINT C] --> C
    C --> D[APPLY INTERSECTION OPERATOR]
    D --> E[FUZZY DECISION SET D]
    E --> F[DEFUZZIFICATION CENTROID MAXIMUM]
    F --> G[CRISP OPTIMAL DECISION X STAR]
    E --> H[ALTERNATIVE: ALPHA CUT ANALYSIS]
    H --> G
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#f0e1ff
    style D fill:#ffe1f0
    style E fill:#e1ffe1
    style F fill:#fff0e1
    style G fill:#ff6347,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Multi-Criteria Fuzzy Decision Making Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ALTERNATIVE SET A1 A2 A3 AN] --> D[FUZZY EVALUATION MATRIX]
    B[CRITERIA SET C1 C2 CN] --> D
    C[CRITERIA WEIGHTS W1 W2 WN] --> D
    D --> E[WEIGHTED AGGREGATION]
    E --> F[FUZZY SCORE S1 S2 SN]
    F --> G[RANKING USING YAGERS INDEX]
    G --> H[BEST ALTERNATIVE A STAR]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#f0e1ff
    style D fill:#ffe1f0
    style E fill:#e1ffe1
    style F fill:#fff0e1
    style G fill:#e1f0ff
    style H fill:#ff6347,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Individual vs Group Fuzzy Decision Making

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FUZZY DECISION MAKING FDM] --> B[TWO PARADIGMS]
    B --> C[INDIVIDUAL FDM]
    B --> D[GROUP FDM]
    C --> E[SINGLE DECISION MAKER]
    C --> F[PERSONAL PREFERENCE FUZZY SETS]
    C --> G[BELLMAN ZADEH DIRECT]
    D --> H[MULTIPLE EXPERTS]
    D --> I[FUZZY AGGREGATION YAGER OWA]
    D --> J[CONFLICT RESOLUTION]
    G --> K[OPTIMAL DECISION]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#e1ffe1
    style D fill:#ffe1f0
    style K fill:#ff6347,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Fuzzy Linear Programming — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FUZZY OBJECTIVE COEFFICIENTS] --> D[FUZZY LP MODEL]
    B[FUZZY CONSTRAINT MATRIX] --> D
    C[FUZZY RESOURCE VECTOR] --> D
    D --> E[CONVERT TO EQUIVALENT CRISP LP]
    E --> F1[METHOD 1: ALPHA CUT DECOMPOSITION]
    E --> F2[METHOD 2: SYMMETRIC MODEL BELLMAN ZADEH]
    E --> F3[METHOD 3: VERMEERS APPROACH]
    F1 --> G[CRISP LP SOLVER SIMPLEX]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[OPTIMAL FUZZY SOLUTION X STAR]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#f0e1ff
    style D fill:#ffe1f0
    style E fill:#e1ffe1
    style G fill:#fff0e1
    style H fill:#ff6347,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.5 Defuzzification Methods — Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FUZZY DECISION SET] --> B[DEFUZZIFICATION TECHNIQUE]
    B --> C[CENTROID METHOD COG]
    B --> D[MEAN OF MAXIMUM MOM]
    B --> E[CENTER OF AREA COA]
    B --> F[WEIGHTED AVERAGE]
    C --> G[CRISP OUTPUT X STAR]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#e1ffe1
    style D fill:#ffe1f0
    style E fill:#f0e1ff
    style F fill:#fff0e1
    style G fill:#ff6347,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**  
> **Q: Define Fuzzy Decision Making. State the Bellman–Zadeh formulation.** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Fuzzy Decision Making is a decision-theoretic framework in which the goals, constraints, and outcomes of a decision are represented as fuzzy sets, and the optimal decision is obtained by aggregating these fuzzy sets.

According to Bellman and Zadeh (1970), if $G$ is a fuzzy goal and $C$ is a fuzzy constraint defined on the universe $X$, then the fuzzy decision $D$ is given by:

$$\mu_D(x) = \mu_G(x) \wedge \mu_C(x) = \min\{\mu_G(x), \mu_C(x)\}$$

The optimal decision is the value $x^* \in X$ that maximizes $\mu_D(x)$:

$$x^* = \arg\max_{x \in X} \mu_D(x)$$

> *(Examiner's note: A clear definition + correct formula + 1-line interpretation = full 3 marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**  
> **Q: Differentiate between Fuzzy Goal and Fuzzy Constraint with an example.** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fuzzy Goal | Fuzzy Constraint |
|--------|------------|------------------|
| **Meaning** | Expresses *desired* outcomes | Expresses *feasible/allowable* outcomes |
| **Interpretation** | "What we want" | "What we can accept" |
| **Example (Loan Approval)** | "Maximize customer satisfaction" with $\mu_G(\text{satisfaction})$ | "Risk should be low" with $\mu_C(\text{risk})$ |
| **Role in Decision** | Filters desirability | Filters feasibility |
| **Mathematical Treatment** | Identical — both are fuzzy sets on $X$ | Identical — combined via min operator |

In Bellman–Zadeh, both are treated as *equivalent fuzzy sets*, which is the elegance of the framework.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B-A | 14 Marks]**  
> **Question A:**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Bellman–Zadeh approach** to fuzzy decision making with a suitable numerical example. Discuss how the approach can be extended to multiple fuzzy goals and fuzzy constraints. **[7 Marks]**
> **(b)** Describe **Multi-Criteria Decision Making (MCDM)** in a fuzzy environment. Illustrate with a suitable engineering example and show how the optimal alternative is selected using weighted aggregation. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — [7 Marks]

**1. Introduction to Bellman–Zadeh Approach [2 marks]:**

The Bellman–Zadeh (1970) approach unifies the treatment of goals and constraints in decision making under fuzziness. In a fuzzy environment, the decision maker's *goal* and the *constraint* are both represented as fuzzy sets on the universe of discourse $X$.

- **Fuzzy Goal $G$:** Describes the degree of satisfaction with each $x \in X$.
- **Fuzzy Constraint $C$:** Describes the degree of feasibility of each $x \in X$.

**2. Mathematical Formulation [2 marks]:**

The fuzzy decision set $D$ is defined as the intersection:

$$\mu_D(x) = \mu_G(x) \wedge \mu_C(x) = \min\{\mu_G(x), \mu_C(x)\}$$

The optimal crisp decision is:

$$x^* = \arg\max_{x \in X} \mu_D(x)$$

**3. Numerical Example [2 marks]:**

Consider $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ with:

| $x$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mu_G(x)$ | 0.1 | 0.4 | 0.7 | 1.0 | 0.8 | 0.5 |
| $\mu_C(x)$ | 0.6 | 0.9 | 1.0 | 0.9 | 0.6 | 0.2 |

Computing $\mu_D(x) = \min(\mu_G, \mu_C)$:

| $x$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mu_D(x)$ | 0.1 | 0.4 | 0.7 | **0.9** | 0.6 | 0.2 |

The optimal decision is $x^* = 4$ with $\mu_D = 0.9$.

**4. Extension to Multiple Goals and Constraints [1 mark]:**

For $n$ fuzzy goals and $m$ fuzzy constraints:

$$\mu_D(x) = \min\{\mu_{G_1}(x), \ldots, \mu_{G_n}(x), \mu_{C_1}(x), \ldots, \mu_{C_m}(x)\}$$

The optimal decision is again obtained by maximizing $\mu_D(x)$.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — [7 Marks]

**1. Definition of Fuzzy MCDM [2 marks]:**

Multi-Criteria Decision Making (MCDM) involves selecting the best alternative from a finite set $A = \{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_p\}$ based on multiple, often conflicting criteria $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_q\}$. In a *fuzzy* environment, the performance ratings $\mu_{ik}$ and/or the criteria weights $w_k$ are fuzzy numbers.

**2. Mathematical Framework [2 marks]:**

Given:
- Alternatives: $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_p$
- Criteria: $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_q$ with weights $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_q$
- Fuzzy evaluation matrix $[\mu_{ik}]$

The aggregated fuzzy score of alternative $A_i$ is:

$$S_i = \sum_{k=1}^{q} w_k \cdot \mu_{ik}$$

The optimal alternative is:

$$A^* = \arg\max_{i} S_i$$

**3. Engineering Example — Supplier Selection [2 marks]:**

A manufacturing firm must select a supplier among $A_1, A_2, A_3$ based on *Quality* (weight 0.5), *Cost* (weight 0.3), and *Delivery Time* (weight 0.2).

| Criterion | Weight $w_k$ | $A_1$ | $A_2$ | $A_3$ |
|-----------|--------------|-------|-------|-------|
| Quality | 0.5 | 0.9 | 0.7 | 0.8 |
| Cost | 0.3 | 0.6 | 0.8 | 0.7 |
| Delivery | 0.2 | 0.7 | 0.6 | 0.9 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= (0.5)(0.9) + (0.3)(0.6) + (0.2)(0.7) = 0.45 + 0.18 + 0.14 = 0.77 \\
S_2 &= (0.5)(0.7) + (0.3)(0.8) + (0.2)(0.6) = 0.35 + 0.24 + 0.12 = 0.71 \\
S_3 &= (0.5)(0.8) + (0.3)(0.7) + (0.2)(0.9) = 0.40 + 0.21 + 0.18 = 0.79
\end{aligned}
$$

**4. Conclusion [1 mark]:**

$A^* = A_3$ with $S_3 = 0.79$ is selected as the best supplier.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B-B | 14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**  
> **Question B:**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **fuzzy decision support system**. Describe the role of fuzzification, fuzzy decision logic, and defuzzification in the system. **[7 Marks]**
> **(b)** What is **fuzzy ranking**? Explain Yager's ranking index and centroid method for ranking fuzzy numbers. Apply both methods to rank the fuzzy numbers $\tilde{A} = (1, 3, 5)$ and $\tilde{B} = (2, 4, 6)$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — [7 Marks]

**1. Block Diagram of Fuzzy Decision Support System [2 marks]:**

```
[CRISP INPUTS] → [FUZZIFICATION] → [FUZZY KNOWLEDGE BASE + RULE BASE] 
                                          ↓
                              [FUZZY DECISION / INFERENCE ENGINE]
                                          ↓
                                 [DEFUZZIFICATION] → [CRISP DECISION]
```

**2. Role of Each Block [3 marks]:**

- **Fuzzification Module:** Converts crisp inputs (e.g., sensor readings, numerical scores) into membership values across predefined fuzzy sets (e.g., Low, Medium, High). This is the *interface* between the real world and the fuzzy engine.
- **Fuzzy Knowledge Base + Rule Base:** Stores (i) the membership function definitions for all linguistic variables, and (ii) the IF–THEN rules (e.g., "IF profit is high AND cost is low THEN accept").
- **Fuzzy Decision / Inference Engine:** Performs fuzzy reasoning using the rule base and the fuzzified inputs, applying intersection (min), union (max), and implication operators to derive a fuzzy output.
- **Defuzzification Module:** Converts the aggregated fuzzy output back to a single crisp value using methods such as Centroid, Mean of Maximum, or Center of Area.

**3. Working Example [1 mark]:**

For a loan approval system, inputs such as *income*, *credit score*, and *debt ratio* are fuzzified, combined via rules, and a crisp decision (*approve/reject*) is produced.

**4. Engineering Applications [1 mark]:**

- Medical diagnosis systems
- Stock trading decision systems
- Climate control systems
- Autonomous vehicle decision making

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — [7 Marks]

**1. Definition of Fuzzy Ranking [1 mark]:**

Fuzzy ranking is the process of ordering fuzzy numbers (or fuzzy sets) along the real line, since they cannot be directly compared using standard arithmetic.

**2. Yager's Ranking Index [2 marks]:**

For a fuzzy number $\tilde{A}$ with $\alpha$-cut $A_\alpha = [\underline{a}_\alpha, \bar{a}_\alpha]$:

$$Y(\tilde{A}) = \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{1} (\underline{a}_\alpha + \bar{a}_\alpha) \, d\alpha$$

For a triangular fuzzy number $\tilde{A} = (a, b, c)$, this simplifies to:

$$Y(\tilde{A}) = \frac{a + 2b + c}{4}$$

**3. Centroid (Center of Gravity) Method [1 mark]:**

$$C(\tilde{A}) = \frac{\int x \cdot \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \, dx}{\int \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \, dx}$$

For a triangular fuzzy number $\tilde{A} = (a, b, c)$:

$$C(\tilde{A}) = \frac{a + b + c}{3}$$

**4. Application to $\tilde{A} = (1, 3, 5)$ and $\tilde{B} = (2, 4, 6)$ [2 marks]:**

**Yager's Index:**

$$Y(\tilde{A}) = \frac{1 + 2(3) + 5}{4} = \frac{12}{4} = 3.0$$

$$Y(\tilde{B}) = \frac{2 + 2(4) + 6}{4} = \frac{16}{4} = 4.0$$

Since $Y(\tilde{B}) > Y(\tilde{A})$, we conclude $\tilde{B} > \tilde{A}$.

**Centroid Method:**

$$C(\tilde{A}) = \frac{1 + 3 + 5}{3} = \frac{9}{3} = 3.0$$

$$C(\tilde{B}) = \frac{2 + 4 + 6}{3} = \frac{12}{3} = 4.0$$

Since $C(\tilde{B}) > C(\tilde{A})$, again $\tilde{B} > \tilde{A}$.

**5. Conclusion [1 mark]:**

Both methods consistently rank $\tilde{B} > \tilde{A}$, confirming that $\tilde{B}$ is the larger fuzzy number.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take the argmax:** Many students compute $\mu_D(x)$ correctly but forget to identify $x^* = \arg\max \mu_D(x)$. This costs **1–2 marks** in numerical problems.
> 2. **Wrong operator:** Using *product* when the question clearly expects *min* (or vice versa) without justification. Always state the operator explicitly.
> 3. **Treating fuzzy goal and constraint differently:** They are *mathematically equivalent* fuzzy sets in Bellman–Zadeh. Do not apply different aggregation rules to each.
> 4. **Skipping the engineering interpretation:** KTU awards 1 mark in most numerical problems for stating the *practical meaning* of the answer. Always close with "Therefore, the company should produce 400 units" or equivalent.
> 5. **Confusing Yager's index with centroid:** They give the same value for *symmetric* triangular fuzzy numbers but differ for asymmetric ones. Know both formulas.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bellman–Zadeh formulation** is the cornerstone: $\mu_D(x) = \mu_G(x) \wedge \mu_C(x)$, with $x^* = \arg\max \mu_D(x)$.
- **Fuzzy goal and fuzzy constraint are symmetric** — both are fuzzy sets on the same universe, combined by intersection.
- **The minimum (min) operator** is the most common in KTU answers; mention *product* as an alternative.
- **Extension to multiple goals/constraints** uses the same min-based aggregation: $\mu_D(x) = \min_{i,j}\{\mu_{G_i}, \mu_{C_j}\}$.
- **Multi-Criteria Decision Making (MCDM)** uses weighted aggregation: $S_i = \sum_{k=1}^{q} w_k \cdot \mu_{ik}$.
- **Yager's Index** for triangular fuzzy number $\tilde{A} = (a, b, c)$: $Y(\tilde{A}) = (a + 2b + c)/4$.
- **Centroid** for triangular fuzzy number $\tilde{A} = (a, b, c)$: $C(\tilde{A}) = (a + b + c)/3$.
- **Fuzzy Linear Programming** extends classical LP by allowing fuzzy coefficients in objective and constraints; converted to crisp LP via α-cuts.
- **Group decision making** uses aggregation operators like *Ordered Weighted Averaging (OWA)*, *Yager's operator*, and *fuzzy majority*.
- **Defuzzification methods** include Centroid, Mean of Maximum, Center of Area, and Weighted Average.
- **α-cut decomposition** is a powerful technique to convert a fuzzy LP into a family of crisp LPs.
- **The decision support system architecture** has 4 blocks: Fuzzification → Knowledge Base → Inference Engine → Defuzzification.
- **Always interpret the result** in the engineering context of the problem.
- **Know the difference between symmetric and asymmetric triangular fuzzy numbers** — they affect Yager's and Centroid values differently.
- **Engineering applications to mention** in descriptive answers: medical diagnosis, financial decisions, supplier selection, manufacturing, autonomous systems, climate control, and load dispatch in power systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Version of Genetic Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT521 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Version of Genetic Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** is a population-based, stochastic, metaheuristic optimization technique inspired by the biological mechanisms of natural selection and genetics, first formally proposed by **John Holland (1975)** and rigorously developed by **David E. Goldberg (1989)**. In KTU 2024 Soft Computing parlance, the *basic version* refers to the canonical/simple GA that operates on fixed-length binary-encoded chromosomes using three core genetic operators — **Selection, Crossover, and Mutation** — iterated over successive generations until a termination condition is met.

Formally, a basic GA maintains a population

$$P(t) = \{x_1(t), x_2(t), \dots, x_N(t)\}$$

at iteration $t$, and produces a new population $P(t+1)$ by applying selection, crossover, and mutation, with the goal of maximising (or minimising) an objective function $f(x)$ known as the *fitness function*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The term "Basic Version" specifically means the *Simple GA / Canonical GA* introduced by Goldberg. It does *not* include advanced variants such as Adaptive GA, Elitist GA, Steady-State GA, or Messy GA.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Survivor Island"

Imagine a deserted island with **N survivors**, each with a unique combination of survival traits (strength, intelligence, agility encoded as a binary string — `1010110...`). Each generation:

1. A **fitness test** measures how well each survivor can gather food.
2. The **fittest survivors** are *selected* as parents (nature's bias toward the strong).
3. Parents **mate (crossover)** — their child inherits a mix of their trait-bits.
4. A few random **mutations** occur — a child unexpectedly runs faster (one bit flips).
5. The next generation replaces the old, and the cycle repeats.

Over hundreds of generations, the population converges toward survivors that are *optimally* adapted to the environment — mirroring how GA converges toward an optimal (or near-optimal) solution to a problem.

## 1.3 Terminology Mapping Table

| Biological Term | Genetic Algorithm Equivalent | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Individual organism | Candidate solution | $x_i$ |
| Chromosome | Encoded solution string | Binary / Real vector |
| Gene | Decision variable / bit | $g_k$ |
| Allele | Value of a gene | $0$ or $1$ |
| Population | Set of candidate solutions | $P(t)$ |
| Fitness | Quality of solution | $f(x_i)$ |
| Generation | Iteration of the algorithm | $t$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The basic GA assumes *fixed-length* chromosomes, *binary encoding* (in its classical form), and a *probabilistic* — not deterministic — selection mechanism. These assumptions are the bedrock of Holland's **Schema Theorem**.

## 1.4 Why Basic GA is Important in Soft Computing

- **Derivative-free**: Does not require gradient information, suitable for discontinuous, non-differentiable, or black-box objective functions.
- **Parallel search**: Works on a population, reducing the risk of local optima trapping.
- **Domain-independent**: Same algorithm applies to scheduling, routing, NN training, fuzzy controller tuning, etc.
- **Theoretical foundation**: The **Schema Theorem** and **Building Block Hypothesis** mathematically justify *why* simple GA works — a frequent KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Structure of Basic GA — Step-by-Step Logic

The basic version of GA proceeds through a strict iterative loop. The following bullet list is the **gold standard** for answering KTU 14-mark descriptive questions.

**Step 1 — Initialisation**
- Generate a population $P(0)$ of $N$ individuals.
- Each individual $x_i$ is a binary string of length $l$.
- Typically uniform random: each bit $g_k \in \{0, 1\}$ with probability $0.5$.

**Step 2 — Fitness Evaluation**
- Decode each chromosome and compute $f(x_i)$.
- For *minimisation* problems, convert to a maximisation form:
  $$F(x_i) = \frac{1}{1 + f(x_i)} \quad \text{or} \quad F(x_i) = C_{\max} - f(x_i)$$

**Step 3 — Termination Check**
- Stop if any of these conditions are met:
  - Maximum generations $T_{\max}$ reached.
  - Convergence: best fitness stabilised over $k$ generations.
  - Satisfactory fitness threshold attained.

**Step 4 — Selection (Reproduction)**
- Choose parent chromosomes with probability proportional to fitness.
- The most popular operator is **Roulette Wheel Selection**.

**Step 5 — Crossover (Recombination)**
- With probability $p_c$ (typical $0.6$ to $0.9$), combine two parents to form offspring.
- Basic version uses **Single-Point Crossover**.

**Step 6 — Mutation**
- With probability $p_m$ (typical $0.001$ to $0.05$), flip individual bits of the offspring.
- Provides *genetic diversity* and prevents premature convergence.

**Step 7 — Replacement**
- Form the new population $P(t+1)$ from selected/crossovered/mutated offspring.
- Go to Step 2.

## 2.2 The Three Genetic Operators — Engineering Perspective

### 2.2.1 Selection
Selection pressure drives the population toward higher-fitness regions of the search space. The basic GA uses **fitness-proportionate selection**.

### 2.2.2 Crossover
Crossover exploits the *building blocks* already discovered. It does not create new alleles; it combines existing successful patterns.

### 2.2.3 Mutation
Mutation is the *exploration engine*. It introduces new alleles that may not exist in the current population, ensuring long-term diversity.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive heuristic:** Crossover = *Exploitation* (refining good solutions), Mutation = *Exploration* (discovering new regions). A well-tuned GA balances both.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Population size | $N$ | Number of individuals | $20 \le N \le 200$ |
| 2 | Chromosome length | $l$ | Number of bits/genes | Problem dependent |
| 3 | Fitness of individual $i$ | $f_i$ | Objective value | $\mathbb{R}^+$ |
| 4 | Average population fitness | $\bar{f} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i$ | Mean scalar | — |
| 5 | Selection probability | $p_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$ | Dimensionless probability | $0 \le p_i \le 1$ |
| 6 | Expected count of selected copies | $e_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\bar{f}}$ | Dimensionless | $e_i \ge 1$ for above-average |
| 7 | Crossover probability | $p_c$ | Dimensionless | $0.6 \le p_c \le 0.9$ |
| 8 | Mutation probability | $p_m$ | Dimensionless | $0.001 \le p_m \le 0.05$ |
| 9 | Schema defining length | $\delta(H)$ | Distance between first & last fixed position | $0 \le \delta(H) \le l-1$ |
| 10 | Schema order | $o(H)$ | Number of fixed positions | $0 \le o(H) \le l$ |
| 11 | **Schema Theorem (Holland)** | $E[m(H,t+1)] \ge m(H,t) \cdot \dfrac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \dfrac{\delta(H)}{l-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]$ | Growth rate of schema $H$ | Key KTU formula |
| 12 | Convergence criterion | $\vert f_{\text{best}}(t) - f_{\text{best}}(t-k) \vert \le \epsilon$ | Absolute change in best fitness | User-defined $\epsilon$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown safety:** In the table above, absolute value is written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ (LaTeX) to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax. **Do not** write raw `|x|` inside table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP):** Chromosome = permutation of cities; operators = partially-mapped crossover.
- **Neural Network weight training:** Chromosome = concatenated weight vector; fitness = inverse MSE.
- **Job-shop & exam scheduling:** Each chromosome = a candidate timetable.
- **Fuzzy logic controller design:** GA tunes membership function parameters and rule base.
- **VLSI circuit partitioning & routing:** Combinatorial layout optimisation.

The **basic version** is rarely deployed in production today — it serves as the *conceptual building block* upon which advanced variants (Elitist GA, CHC, Adaptive GA, Multi-Objective NSGA-II) are constructed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Roulette Wheel Selection Probability

**Statement:** The probability that individual $x_i$ is selected as a parent is proportional to its fitness $f_i$.

**Derivation:**

Let the total fitness of the population be

$$F_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j$$

The total "roulette wheel" is normalised to the interval $[0, 1]$ by dividing the wheel arc of each individual by the total arc.

For individual $i$, the arc length (or probability of selection) is therefore

$$p_i = \frac{f_i}{F_{\text{total}}} = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$$

The expected number of copies of individual $i$ in the next generation's mating pool, after selecting $N$ parents, is

$$e_i = N \cdot p_i = N \cdot \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $N$:

$$e_i = \frac{f_i}{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} = \frac{f_i}{\bar{f}}$$

This is the **expected selection count** used by Goldberg in his textbook. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> When fitness values are negative or span several orders of magnitude, **scaling** is required — common techniques are *linear scaling*, *sigma truncation*, and *windowing*. The *basic* GA assumes non-negative fitness and no scaling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Single-Point Crossover Disruption Probability

**Statement:** A schema $H$ of defining length $\delta(H)$ in a chromosome of length $l$ is *disrupted* by single-point crossover if the crossover point falls strictly between the first and last fixed positions of $H$.

**Derivation:**

The number of possible crossover sites is $l - 1$ (cannot cut at either end and preserve a meaningful segment of length $> 0$).

The defining length $\delta(H)$ is defined as the distance between the *first* and *last* fixed positions of the schema:

$$\delta(H) = \text{last}(H) - \text{first}(H)$$

The number of crossover points that *destroy* schema $H$ equals $\delta(H)$.

Therefore, the probability that a *single* crossover event disrupts $H$ is

$$P_{\text{disrupt}} = \frac{\delta(H)}{l - 1}$$

The probability of *survival* through crossover is

$$P_{\text{survive}} = 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{l - 1}$$

assuming crossover occurs with probability $p_c$. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's insight:** Notice that *short* schemas (small $\delta(H)$) survive more easily than long ones. This is the formal reason why **short, low-order, above-average schemas** (the *building blocks*) multiply exponentially — the foundation of the Schema Theorem.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Holland's Schema Theorem (Foundational)

**Statement:** Short, low-order, above-average schemas grow exponentially under basic GA.

**Derivation:**

The expected number of schema $H$ in generation $t+1$, considering all three operators, is

$$E[m(H, t+1)] = m(H, t) \cdot \underbrace{\frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}}}_{\text{Selection}} \cdot \underbrace{\left[1 - p_c \frac{\delta(H)}{l-1}\right]}_{\text{Crossover survival}} \cdot \underbrace{(1 - p_m)^{o(H)}}_{\text{Mutation survival}}$$

The mutation term uses the fact that a schema $H$ of order $o(H)$ survives mutation only if **all** its fixed positions remain unchanged. The probability of *one* fixed position surviving mutation is $(1 - p_m)$, so for $o(H)$ independent positions it is $(1 - p_m)^{o(H)}$.

Using the inequality $(1 - p_m)^{o(H)} \ge 1 - p_m \cdot o(H)$ (valid for small $p_m$, by Bernoulli's bound $1 - x \le (1 - x)^n \le 1$ for $0 \le x \le 1$ … actually the correct bound is $(1 - p_m)^{o(H)} \ge 1 - p_m \cdot o(H)$ for $0 \le p_m \le 1$), we obtain Holland's inequality:

$$E[m(H, t+1)] \ge m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \frac{\delta(H)}{l-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]$$

This is the **Fundamental Theorem of Genetic Algorithms (FTGA)**. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A schema $H$ grows exponentially across generations if
> $$\frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \frac{\delta(H)}{l-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right] > 1$$
> i.e., it is *above-average fitness*, *short*, and *low-order*. This justifies the *Building Block Hypothesis* (Goldberg, 1989).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Style)

**Problem:** A population of 5 chromosomes has fitness values $f = \{10, 20, 30, 40, 50\}$.
Compute the selection probability and expected count of each individual.

**Solution:**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{5} f_j = 10 + 20 + 30 + 40 + 50 = 150$$

$$\bar{f} = \frac{150}{5} = 30$$

| Individual $i$ | $f_i$ | $p_i = f_i / 150$ | $e_i = f_i / \bar{f} = f_i / 30$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | $0.0667$ | $0.333$ |
| 2 | 20 | $0.1333$ | $0.667$ |
| 3 | 30 | $0.2000$ | $1.000$ |
| 4 | 40 | $0.2667$ | $1.333$ |
| 5 | 50 | $0.3333$ | $1.667$ |

Total expected count: $\sum e_i = 0.333 + 0.667 + 1.000 + 1.333 + 1.667 = 5.000 = N$. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** Individual 5 is *most likely* to be selected (probability $33.33\%$), while individual 1 is *least likely* ($6.67\%$). This demonstrates selection pressure in basic GA.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Basic GA | Real-Engineered GA |
|---|---|---|
| Selection | Fitness-proportionate (RWS) | Tournament, Rank-based, SUS |
| Crossover | Single-point | Two-point, Uniform, Order-based |
| Mutation | Bit-flip | Polynomial, Gaussian, Inversion |
| Elitism | Not present | Always preserve best |
| Scaling | None | Linear / Sigma / Boltzmann |
| Termination | Max generations | Convergence + max + target |
| Convergence guarantee | None (stochastic) | Provable in elitist variants |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Flowchart of Basic Genetic Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Initialise Population P of N random chromosomes]
    B --> C[Decode and Evaluate Fitness fxi for all i]
    C --> D{Termination condition met?}
    D -- No --> E[Selection: Choose parents using Roulette Wheel]
    E --> F[Apply Single-Point Crossover with probability pc]
    F --> G[Apply Bit-Flip Mutation with probability pm]
    G --> H[Form Next Generation Pt plus 1]
    H --> C
    D -- Yes --> I[Return Best Chromosome as Solution]
    I --> J([Stop])

    classDef io fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef op  fill:#d9e8f5,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px,color:#0b2545
    classDef dec fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:1px,color:#0b2545
    class A,J io
    class B,C,E,F,G,H op
    class D,I dec
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Basic GA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Encoding Module"
        E1[Problem Space] --> E2[Binary Encoder]
        E2 --> E3[Chromosome String]
    end

    subgraph "Population Module"
        P1[Random Init] --> P2[Population P of t]
    end

    subgraph "Evaluation Module"
        V1[Decoder] --> V2[Fitness Function f]
        V2 --> V3[Fitness Vector]
    end

    subgraph "Reproduction Module"
        R1[Selection: RWS] --> R2[Mating Pool]
        R2 --> R3[Crossover Operator]
        R3 --> R4[Mutation Operator]
    end

    subgraph "Replacement Module"
        S1[Form P of t plus 1] --> S2[Loop back to Evaluation]
    end

    E3 --> P1
    P2 --> V1
    V3 --> R1
    R4 --> S1
    S2 --> V1
```

## 4.3 Schema Theorem Causal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Schema H of length l] --> B{Fitness fH greater than average f bar?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Selection amplifies copies of H]
    B -- No --> D[Selection reduces copies of H]
    C --> E{Defining length deltaH small?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Crossover rarely disrupts H]
    E -- No --> G[Crossover may disrupt H]
    F --> H{Order oH small?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Mutation rarely disrupts H]
    H -- No --> J[Mutation may disrupt H]
    I --> K[H grows exponentially]
    J --> L[H growth uncertain]
    G --> M[H may not survive]
    D --> N[H decays exponentially]

    classDef good fill:#c6efce,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#0b2545
    classDef bad  fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#0b2545
    classDef q    fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#0b2545
    class K good
    class N bad
    class L,M bad
    class B,E,H q
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** The Schema Theorem diagram shows that a schema $H$ experiences *exponential growth* only when **all three** conditions are met — above-average fitness, short defining length, low order. This is the formal justification for why simple GA works on hard combinatorial problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define Genetic Algorithm. List its basic operators.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *Genetic Algorithm (GA)* is a population-based stochastic optimisation technique inspired by the principles of natural selection and genetics, used to find approximate solutions to optimisation and search problems.

The **basic version** of GA uses three genetic operators:

1. **Selection (Reproduction):** Probabilistic selection of parent chromosomes proportional to fitness — e.g., Roulette Wheel Selection.
2. **Crossover (Recombination):** Exchange of genetic material between two parents — e.g., Single-Point Crossover.
3. **Mutation:** Random alteration of one or more genes — e.g., Bit-Flip Mutation.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing the three operators with one-line description.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023] What is a schema? Give an example. What is the *order* and *defining length* of a schema?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *schema* is a template formed by adding a special don't-care symbol `*` to the binary alphabet, used to describe a subset of chromosomes with similarities at certain positions.

**Example:** Consider the schema $H = 1*01*0$ over a length-6 chromosome.

- **Order $o(H)$:** Number of *fixed* (non-`*`) positions. Here, $o(H) = 4$ (positions 1, 3, 4, 6).
- **Defining length $\delta(H)$:** Distance between first and last fixed positions. $\delta(H) = 6 - 1 = 5$.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for order and defining length computation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** (a) Explain the basic structure of Genetic Algorithm with a neat flowchart. (7 marks)
> (b) Explain the Roulette Wheel Selection method in detail with a suitable example. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Basic structure of GA — 7-mark model answer:**

The basic GA proceeds iteratively over generations, maintaining a population of candidate solutions.

**Step 1 — Initialisation:** A population $P(0)$ of $N$ individuals is created randomly, each chromosome being a binary string of length $l$.

**Step 2 — Fitness Evaluation:** Each individual is decoded and its fitness $f(x_i)$ is evaluated using a problem-specific objective function. For minimisation, a transformation is applied to convert to a maximisation problem.

**Step 3 — Termination Check:** The algorithm stops if the maximum number of generations $T_{\max}$ is reached, or if the best fitness stabilises within a tolerance $\epsilon$, or if a target fitness is achieved.

**Step 4 — Selection:** Parent chromosomes are selected with probability $p_i = f_i / \sum f_j$ using the **Roulette Wheel** method.

**Step 5 — Crossover:** With probability $p_c$, two parents are mated at a randomly chosen crossover point, producing two offspring that inherit a mix of parental bits.

**Step 6 — Mutation:** With probability $p_m$, each bit of an offspring is flipped — providing genetic diversity.

**Step 7 — Replacement:** The new generation $P(t+1)$ replaces $P(t)$, and the algorithm loops back to Step 2.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 for the flowchart. 1 mark for flow, 1 mark each for any 5 steps clearly described, 1 mark for the looping nature.)*

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Roulette Wheel Selection — 7-mark model answer:**

**Concept:** The fitness of each individual defines a *sector area* on a virtual roulette wheel. The wheel is spun $N$ times, and the sector under the pointer determines the selected parent.

**Mathematical formulation:** For a population of $N$ individuals with fitness $f_i$:

$$p_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} \quad , \quad e_i = N \cdot p_i = \frac{f_i}{\bar{f}}$$

**Worked example (5 individuals, fitness $\{10, 20, 30, 40, 50\}$):**

| $i$ | $f_i$ | $p_i$ | $e_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | $0.067$ | $0.333$ |
| 2 | 20 | $0.133$ | $0.667$ |
| 3 | 30 | $0.200$ | $1.000$ |
| 4 | 40 | $0.267$ | $1.333$ |
| 5 | 50 | $0.333$ | $1.667$ |

Cumulative probabilities: $0.067, 0.200, 0.400, 0.667, 1.000$.

**Spin procedure:** Generate uniform random $r \in [0, 1]$. Select the individual whose cumulative range contains $r$.

**Example spin:** If $r = 0.45$, then individual 4 is selected (cumulative range $0.400$ to $0.667$).

**Strengths:** Simple, fitness-proportionate, gives above-average individuals higher reproductive chances.
**Weaknesses:** Premature convergence when fitness values are dominated by one super-individual; *stagnation* when fitness values are nearly equal. **Stochastic Universal Sampling (SUS)** and **Tournament Selection** are common upgrades.

*(1 mark for concept, 1 mark for formula, 2 marks for example, 1 mark strengths, 1 mark weaknesses, 1 mark conclusion.)*

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2022]** (a) Explain the various crossover techniques used in basic Genetic Algorithm. (7 marks)
> (b) Explain mutation operators and discuss the importance of mutation in GA. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Crossover techniques — 7-mark model answer:**

Crossover is the primary *exploitation* operator, combining building blocks from two parent chromosomes to produce offspring.

**1. Single-Point Crossover:**
A single crossover point is chosen randomly between positions $1$ and $l-1$. The bits to the right of the point are exchanged.
- Example: Parents `1010 1101` and `0110 0010`, cut at position 4 → Offspring `1010 0010` and `0110 1101`.

**2. Two-Point Crossover:**
Two crossover points are chosen; the segment between them is exchanged.
- Example: Cut at positions 3 and 6, exchanging the middle segment.

**3. Uniform Crossover:**
Each bit position is independently chosen from either parent with probability $0.5$. Provides maximum mixing but can disrupt building blocks.

**4. Multi-Point / $k$-Point Crossover:**
Generalisation of two-point; $k$ cut points yield $k+1$ segments that are alternately exchanged.

**Comparison:**

| Technique | Building Block Preservation | Disruption |
|---|---|---|
| Single-Point | High | Low |
| Two-Point | Moderate | Moderate |
| Uniform | Low | High |

Crossover is applied with probability $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$. With probability $1 - p_c$, the parents are copied unchanged into the offspring pool.

*(2 marks each for first three techniques, 1 mark for parameter $p_c$.)*

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Mutation and its importance — 7-mark model answer:**

**Bit-Flip Mutation (basic):**
For each bit of every offspring, with probability $p_m$ (typically $0.001$ to $0.05$), flip the bit value.

**Example:** Offspring `10101010` with mutation at position 3 → `10001010`.

**Probability of all bits surviving mutation:**
For a chromosome of length $l$ with mutation probability $p_m$ per bit:

$$P_{\text{survive}} = (1 - p_m)^l$$

**For small $p_m$:** $P_{\text{survive}} \approx 1 - l \cdot p_m$.

**Importance of Mutation:**

1. **Maintains genetic diversity** — prevents premature convergence to a local optimum.
2. **Recovers lost alleles** — if all chromosomes converge to `1` at a particular bit, only mutation can reintroduce `0`.
3. **Enables exploration of new regions** of the search space.
4. **Complements crossover** — crossover exploits existing patterns; mutation explores new ones.
5. **Critical for infinite search spaces** — where exhaustive search is impossible.

**Why low probability?** Too high $p_m$ degrades GA to a random search, destroying the accumulated building blocks. Goldberg recommends the **$1/l$ rule of thumb**: $p_m \approx 1/l$.

*(2 marks for description, 1 mark for probability, 3 marks for importance points, 1 mark for tuning rule.)*

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing pitfalls in GA questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing fitness function with objective function.** Fitness is a *maximisation-form* measure; objective can be minimisation. Always state the conversion.
> 2. **Forgetting the loop.** Many students describe the steps linearly without emphasising that the algorithm *loops* between Step 2 and Step 7. **1 mark lost.**
> 3. **Schema order vs defining length confusion.** Order = number of fixed positions; defining length = distance between first and last fixed positions. **Easy 1-mark trap.**
> 4. **Roulette Wheel formula missing denominator.** Students often write only $p_i = f_i$ without the normalisation $\sum f_j$. **2 marks lost.**
> 5. **Not stating the typical ranges** of $p_c$ and $p_m$. Examiners reward *engineering awareness*.
> 6. **Missing Schema Theorem inequality sign** — the formula is a *lower bound* ($\ge$), not equality.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Basic GA = population-based stochastic metaheuristic with selection, crossover, mutation.
- **Flow:** Initialise → Evaluate → Select → Crossover → Mutate → Replace → Loop.
- **Roulette Wheel selection probability:** $p_i = f_i / \sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j$.
- **Expected count:** $e_i = f_i / \bar{f}$ (so $\sum e_i = N$).
- **Crossover probability** $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$; **mutation probability** $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$.
- **Schema order** $o(H)$ = number of fixed positions; **defining length** $\delta(H)$ = last $-$ first.
- **Schema Theorem (Holland):** $E[m(H,t+1)] \ge m(H,t) \cdot \dfrac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \dfrac{\delta(H)}{l-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]$.
- **Building Block Hypothesis:** GA works by combining short, low-order, above-average schemas.
- **Crossover types:** Single-point, Two-point, Uniform, Multi-point.
- **Mutation types:** Bit-flip, Swap, Inversion, Scramble.
- **Selection types:** Roulette Wheel, Tournament, Rank-based, SUS, Boltzmann.
- **Basic GA limitations:** Premature convergence, slow on deceptive problems, no convergence guarantee without elitism.
- **Termination:** Max generations, fitness threshold, convergence within $\epsilon$.
- **Encoding:** Binary is canonical; real-coded, permutation, tree encodings exist for specialised problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encoding Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT521 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Encoding Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Soft Computing** and specifically **Genetic Algorithms (GAs)**, **Encoding Methods** (also called *Chromosome Representation* or *Coding Schemes*) refer to the systematic procedure by which the decision variables of a candidate solution to an optimization problem are mapped into a specific data structure (chromosome) that can be processed by the evolutionary operators — selection, crossover, and mutation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Encoding is the process of representing individual solutions (phenotypes) of a problem space as strings (genotypes) of a chosen alphabet so that genetic operators can manipulate them."*

Formally, an encoding is a mapping function $E$ such that:

$$E : \mathcal{P} \rightarrow \mathcal{G}$$

where $\mathcal{P}$ is the **phenotype space** (actual feasible solutions in the real world) and $\mathcal{G}$ is the **genotype space** (encoded representation handled by the GA).

The reverse mapping is the **decoding function** $D$:

$$D : \mathcal{G} \rightarrow \mathcal{P}$$

such that $D(E(p)) \approx p$ for every valid phenotype $p \in \mathcal{P}$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Recipe Translator:**
> Imagine you are a master chef (the *phenotype* — the actual solution) and you have a foreign apprentice who only understands a specific coded language (the *genotype*). The encoding method acts as a **translation rule** — like converting your recipe (a list of ingredients and quantities) into a barcode that the apprentice's machine can read, manipulate, and recombine with other barcodes to invent new recipes.

Another way to think about it: a **DNA strand in biology** carries instructions in a 4-letter alphabet (A, T, G, C). Similarly, in GAs, the chromosome is a string over a chosen alphabet (binary digits, real numbers, integers, or symbols), and the encoding method determines what "alphabet" is used and how the problem variables are packed into that string.

### 1.3 Why Encoding is Critical

The choice of encoding directly determines:
- **Search Space Geometry** — how the algorithm explores solutions.
- **Operator Compatibility** — whether standard crossover/mutation is meaningful.
- **Computational Efficiency** — string length vs. accuracy trade-off.
- **Constraint Handling** — how feasibility of offspring is maintained.

A poorly chosen encoding can make a GA behave like a **random search**, even with high-quality operators.

### 1.4 Key Encoding Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol / Notation | Standard Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Chromosome Length | $L$ | $10$ to $1000$ bits (binary) |
| Precision per Variable | $\epsilon$ | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ |
| Number of Decision Variables | $n$ | $1$ to $100$ |
| Total String Length (Binary) | $L_{total}$ | $n \cdot L_i$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Encoding Methods

There are **five principal encoding schemes** used in GAs, each suited to a particular class of optimization problems:

1. **Binary Encoding**
2. **Real-Valued (Floating-Point) Encoding**
3. **Integer / Discrete Encoding**
4. **Permutation Encoding**
5. **Tree Encoding**

### 2.2 Binary Encoding

- Alphabet: $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$
- Each chromosome is a string of bits.
- Each decision variable is discretized into $L$ bits.

**Decoding formula** (for a variable $x_i \in [a_i, b_i]$ represented by $L_i$ bits):

$$x_i = a_i + \text{decimal}(b_{L_{i-1}+1} \ldots b_{L_i}) \cdot \frac{b_i - a_i}{2^{L_i} - 1}$$

**Precision per variable:**

$$\epsilon_i = \frac{b_i - a_i}{2^{L_i} - 1}$$

**Total chromosome length:**

$$L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i$$

**Advantages:** Simple, well-studied operators, maximum schemata coverage.
**Disadvantages:** Hamming cliff problem (e.g., 0111 → 1000 changes 4 bits for a numerical change of 1), scaling issues for continuous problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hamming Cliff:** A large change in the decoded value causes only a small change in the bit pattern. KTU examiners frequently test this concept.

### 2.3 Real-Valued (Floating-Point) Encoding

- Alphabet: $\Sigma = \mathbb{R}$
- Chromosome = vector $(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

**Example Chromosome:**

$$C = (3.142, \ -1.618, \ 2.718, \ 0.577)$$

**Advantages:** Native representation for continuous optimization, no decoding overhead, no Hamming cliff.
**Disadvantages:** Larger search space, requires specialized operators (BLX-$\alpha$, SBX crossover, polynomial mutation).

### 2.4 Integer / Discrete Encoding

- Alphabet: $\Sigma = \mathbb{Z}$ or a finite discrete set.
- Used when decision variables take **discrete, non-ordered** values (e.g., job IDs, material choices).

**Example:** Selecting a production line configuration:

$$C = (2, \ 5, \ 1, \ 4, \ 3)$$

**Operators:** Standard crossover may produce illegal offspring — special operators like **PMX (Partially Mapped Crossover)** or **OX (Order Crossover)** are used.

### 2.5 Permutation Encoding

- Used for **ordering problems** (TSP, scheduling, routing).
- Each chromosome is a permutation of $n$ distinct integers.
- Constraint: every integer from $1$ to $n$ appears exactly once.

**Example (TSP over 6 cities):**

$$C = (3, \ 1, \ 5, \ 2, \ 6, \ 4)$$

Standard one-point crossover is **illegal** here — it produces duplicates. Special operators: **PMX, OX, CX (Cycle Crossover), Edge Recombination**.

### 2.6 Tree Encoding

- Used in **Genetic Programming (GP)**.
- Chromosome = tree of operators and terminals.
- Internal nodes = functions ($\{+, -, \times, \div, \sin, \cos, \ldots\}$).
- Leaves = variables or constants ($\{x, y, 1, 2, \ldots\}$).

**Example Tree Encoding for the expression $x^2 + 2xy + y$:**

```
        (+)
       /   \
     (*)    (*)
    /  \    /  \
  (x)  (x) (2)  (+)
              /  \
           (*)   (y)
          /  \
        (x)  (y)
```

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Decoding (Binary) | $x_i = a_i + \text{dec}(b_{L_{i-1}+1..L_i}) \cdot \frac{b_i - a_i}{2^{L_i} - 1}$ | dimensionless |
| Precision (Binary) | $\epsilon_i = \frac{b_i - a_i}{2^{L_i} - 1}$ | same as $x_i$ |
| Total bits required | $L_i \geq \log_2 \left(\frac{b_i - a_i}{\epsilon_i} + 1 \right)$ | bits |
| Chromosome length (sum) | $L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i$ | bits |
| Gray decoding | $g_i = b_i \oplus b_{i+1}$ (bitwise XOR) | bits |
| Schema length | $\delta(H) = \text{last\_defined} - \text{first\_defined}$ | bits |
| Schema order | $o(H) = \text{number of fixed positions}$ | count |
| Real-valued operator (BLX-$\alpha$) | $c_k \in [\min(p_k, q_k) - \alpha \cdot d_k, \ \max(p_k, q_k) + \alpha \cdot d_k]$ | real |
| Permutation constraint | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}\{x_i = j\} = 1, \ \forall j \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$ | count |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Binary encoding** is used in VLSI circuit design optimization.
> - **Real-valued** encoding is standard for neural network weight tuning and antenna design.
> - **Permutation** encoding powers the Travelling Salesman Problem solvers used in logistics (Amazon, FedEx).
> - **Tree encoding** drives symbolic regression and automated algorithm discovery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Binary Encoding & Decoding (Full Derivation)

> **Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):**
> A GA encodes a decision variable $x \in [0, 10]$ using $L = 10$ binary bits. A chromosome segment is $b_9 b_8 b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 1011001010$. Decode the value to the actual real number and find the precision.

#### Step 1: Convert the binary string to decimal

The binary string is read MSB-first as the index decreases, so the weight of the $k$-th bit from the right is $2^k$.

$$\text{dec} = \sum_{k=0}^{9} b_k \cdot 2^k$$

Compute term-by-term:

$$\text{dec} = (1 \cdot 2^9) + (0 \cdot 2^8) + (1 \cdot 2^7) + (1 \cdot 2^6) + (0 \cdot 2^5) + (0 \cdot 2^4) + (1 \cdot 2^3) + (0 \cdot 2^2) + (1 \cdot 2^1) + (0 \cdot 2^0)$$

$$\text{dec} = 512 + 0 + 128 + 64 + 0 + 0 + 8 + 0 + 2 + 0$$

$$\text{dec} = 714$$

#### Step 2: Apply the decoding formula

$$x = a + \text{dec} \cdot \frac{b - a}{2^L - 1}$$

Substitute $a = 0$, $b = 10$, $L = 10$, $\text{dec} = 714$:

$$x = 0 + 714 \cdot \frac{10 - 0}{2^{10} - 1}$$

$$x = 714 \cdot \frac{10}{1023}$$

$$x = \frac{7140}{1023}$$

$$x \approx 6.9795$$

#### Step 3: Compute the precision

$$\epsilon = \frac{b - a}{2^L - 1} = \frac{10}{1023} \approx 0.009775 \text{ units}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** The maximum decodable value $1023$ should give $x = 10$. Plugging in: $x = 0 + 1023 \cdot \frac{10}{1023} = 10$ ✓. The minimum $0$ gives $x = 0$ ✓.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Binary Length Required for Given Precision

> **Problem:** How many bits $L$ are needed to encode $x \in [-5, 12]$ with precision $\epsilon \leq 0.001$?

#### Step 1: Set up the precision inequality

$$\epsilon = \frac{b - a}{2^L - 1} \leq 0.001$$

#### Step 2: Solve for $L$

$$2^L - 1 \geq \frac{b - a}{0.001} = \frac{12 - (-5)}{0.001} = \frac{17}{0.001} = 17000$$

$$2^L \geq 17001$$

#### Step 3: Take $\log_2$

$$L \geq \log_2(17001)$$

$$L \geq \frac{\ln(17001)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{9.7409}{0.6931} \approx 14.054$$

#### Step 4: Round up (integer bits required)

$$L = 15 \text{ bits}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Real-Valued BLX-$\alpha$ Crossover

> **Problem:** Two parent chromosomes are $P_1 = (2.0, 5.0)$ and $P_2 = (4.0, 1.0)$. Use BLX-$\alpha$ crossover with $\alpha = 0.5$ to produce an offspring range for each gene.

#### Step 1: Compute gene-wise min, max, and difference

For gene 1:

$$\min_1 = \min(2.0, 4.0) = 2.0, \quad \max_1 = \max(2.0, 4.0) = 4.0, \quad d_1 = 4.0 - 2.0 = 2.0$$

For gene 2:

$$\min_2 = \min(5.0, 1.0) = 1.0, \quad \max_2 = \max(5.0, 1.0) = 5.0, \quad d_2 = 5.0 - 1.0 = 4.0$$

#### Step 2: Compute BLX-$\alpha$ interval

$$I_1 = [2.0 - 0.5 \cdot 2.0, \ 4.0 + 0.5 \cdot 2.0] = [1.0, \ 5.0]$$

$$I_2 = [1.0 - 0.5 \cdot 4.0, \ 5.0 + 0.5 \cdot 4.0] = [-1.0, \ 7.0]$$

#### Step 3: Sample offspring within the intervals

A valid offspring could be:

$$C = (3.2, \ 2.8) \quad \text{(uniformly sampled within } I_1 \times I_2 \text{)}$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Gray Code Conversion (Anti Hamming-Cliff)

> **Problem:** Convert binary $1011$ to Gray code.

#### Step 1: MSB is preserved

$$g_3 = b_3 = 1$$

#### Step 2: Each subsequent Gray bit is XOR of two adjacent binary bits

$$g_2 = b_3 \oplus b_2 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1$$

$$g_1 = b_2 \oplus b_1 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1$$

$$g_0 = b_1 \oplus b_0 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$$

#### Step 3: Final Gray code

$$G = 1110$$

**Verification:** Adjacent Gray codes differ by exactly 1 bit — eliminates the Hamming cliff.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Encoding Selection Guide

| Problem Type | Recommended Encoding | Reason | Key Operator |
|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous smooth function | Real-valued | No decoding error | SBX, Polynomial mutation |
| Discrete combinatorial | Integer | Native to problem | Custom uniform crossover |
| Ordering / routing | Permutation | Preserves uniqueness | PMX, OX, ER |
| Symbolic regression | Tree | Evolves expressions | Subtree crossover, hoist mutation |
| Binary decisions (yes/no) | Binary | Natural mapping | 1-point, 2-point crossover |
| High-precision engineering | Binary + Gray | Smooth fitness landscape | Bit-flip mutation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Encoding-Decoding Pipeline (Mermaid Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Problem Space Phenotype P"] --> B["Encoding Function E"]
    B --> C["Genotype Space G Chromosome String"]
    C --> D["Genetic Operators Crossover Mutation"]
    D --> E["Offspring Genotype Gprime"]
    E --> F["Decoding Function D"]
    F --> G["Candidate Solution Pprime"]
    G --> H{"Feasible and Better Fitness"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Accept into New Population"]
    H -- No --> J["Discard or Penalize"]
    I --> K["Next Generation Population"]
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Encoding Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Encoding Methods in GAs"] --> B["Binary Encoding"]
    A --> C["Real Valued Encoding"]
    A --> D["Integer Discrete Encoding"]
    A --> E["Permutation Encoding"]
    A --> F["Tree Encoding"]
    B --> B1["Standard Binary"]
    B --> B2["Gray Code"]
    B --> B3["Excess Code"]
    C --> C1["Float Vector"]
    C --> C2["Log Coded"]
    D --> D1["Discrete Set Coded"]
    D --> D2["Categorical Coded"]
    E --> E1["Traveling Salesman"]
    E --> E2["Job Shop Scheduling"]
    F --> F1["Genetic Programming Trees"]
    F --> F2["LISP S Expressions"]
```

### 4.3 Chromosome Structure Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CHROM["Chromosome for n Variables Binary Encoding"]
        direction LR
        V1["x1 in a1 b1 - L1 bits"] --> V2["x2 in a2 b2 - L2 bits"] --> V3["x3 in a3 b3 - L3 bits"] --> VN["xn in an bn - Ln bits"]
    end
    OP1["Gene 1"] -.-> V1
    OP2["Gene 2"] -.-> V2
    OP3["Gene 3"] -.-> V3
    OPN["Gene n"] -.-> VN
```

### 4.4 Crossover Operator Mapping per Encoding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Parent Chromosome"] --> OP["Encoding Type"]
    OP --> Q1["Binary - One Point Two Point Uniform"]
    OP --> Q2["Real - BLX Alpha SBX"]
    OP --> Q3["Integer - PMX OX Cycle"]
    OP --> Q4["Tree - Subtree Swap"]
    OP --> Q5["Permutation - Edge Recombination"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INI["Initialization Phase"]
        I1["Define Decision Variables and Bounds"] --> I2["Choose Encoding Method"]
        I2 --> I3["Calculate Chromosome Length L"]
        I3 --> I4["Generate Random Initial Population"]
    end
    I4 --> EV1
    subgraph EVA["Evaluation Phase"]
        EV1["Decode Each Chromosome to Phenotype"]
        EV1 --> EV2["Compute Fitness Function"]
    end
    EV2 --> OP1

        OP1["Selection Roulette Wheel Tournament"]
        OP1 --> OP2["Crossover Encoding Specific"]
        OP2 --> OP3["Mutation Encoding Specific"]
    end
    OP3 --> TERM{"Termination Criterion Met"}
    TERM -- No --> I4
    TERM -- Yes --> OUT["Report Best Solution"]
```
